name: .lumen (dotLumen) Glasses
one_liner: Guide-dog-replacement headset that computes safe walking paths 100x/sec and steers the user via forehead haptics.
funding: ~€10M total (€5M Jul-2024 + €5M Jan-2025 seed); CB Insights/PitchBook ~$5.25M reported. Note: also pivoting toward autonomous delivery robots (€11M round reported).
form_factor: Bulky AR/VR-style headset (~1 kg): 6 cameras, 3 IMUs, 2 laser projectors, forehead vibration motors + rear battery.
target_users: Legally blind adults wanting independent outdoor navigation without cane/guide dog.
key_features: Self-driving-style perception, full-path guidance to saved/new destinations, street crossing, obstacle avoidance above/below ground, haptic+audio cues. CE-certified, pursuing Class I medical device + FDA. 400+ testers in 40+ countries. CES 2026 public demo.
why:
Real, shipping company — confirmed via primary source (dotlumen.com) and reputable press. BUT it occupies the opposite corner of the market from us on every axis that matters:

PRICE: ~€9,999 / ~$11,500 one-time. We target sub-$700. They are ~16x our price — a different buyer entirely (heavily reliant on government assistive-tech reimbursement in Romania). They do NOT compete for our price-sensitive freemium user.

FORM FACTOR: ~1 kg forehead-mounted AR/VR-style headset ("half-faced motorcycle helmet," large rear battery, 6 cameras + 2 IR laser projectors + 3 IMUs), ~2 hr battery. We are a ~30g glasses frame + neck-worn compute box. Their bulk is a major adoption barrier; our lightweight split form is a direct differentiator.

OFFLINE/SAFETY: Validated, not refuted — they are also fully offline/on-device. CEO explicitly: "We cannot risk the latency... between the Cloud and a remote device" when crossing streets. So our "offline = mobility safety" thesis is correct and shared by the most credible player; it is table stakes, not a moat against them. Our moat vs them is price + weight + freemium, NOT offline.

WEDGE OVERLAP: Both do mobility (obstacle avoidance + path guidance). They go further — turn-by-turn navigation to saved/new destinations, street crossing, no-GPS dead-reckoning. Functionally the most advanced mobility competitor. But because of the 16x price and headset bulk, they serve a premium, reimbursement-funded niche; minimal head-to-head overlap with our affordable mass-market freemium segment. Hence overlap downgraded high → LOW.

PIVOT NOTE (confirmed, important): The €11M is a Romanian government GRANT (PABLO project, Sep 2025–Sep 2028) to build autonomous humanoid/quadruped DELIVERY ROBOTS, reusing their PAD AI stack. Glasses continue in parallel (CES 2026 award, Arrow Electronics production scaling, national roadshow) — so NOT a dead product. But it signals their strategic center of gravity is drifting toward the far larger delivery-robotics TAM, which reduces their long-run focus/threat on the blind-mobility market we're entering.

NET: A credible, technically excellent incumbent that validates our core thesis (offline mobility-safety) while leaving our actual market (affordable, lightweight, freemium) wide open. Low direct threat; useful as proof the category is real and as the premium reference point we undercut. No subscription, so we can't out-price them on recurring cost — our edge is the upfront price and wearability.
corrections:
1) offline: claim said "partial" → CORRECT to YES. Fully on-device/edge (NVIDIA vision engine, proprietary semantic-segmentation model at 100 fps); CEO explicitly rejects cloud due to street-crossing latency risk. No connectivity/infrastructure dependency.
2) subscription: claim said "unknown" → effectively NO. One-time €9,999 purchase with 24-month warranty; no recurring fee mentioned on product page or any source.
3) price: claim "~$11,800 (€9,999)" is accurate; €9,999 confirmed VAT-included on dotlumen.com. USD figure varies with FX (~$11.5k).
4) overlap_with_us: claim said HIGH → DOWNGRADE to LOW. Same wedge (mobility) but 16x price and 1kg headset put them in a premium/reimbursement niche, not our affordable freemium segment.
5) threat_level: claim said MEDIUM → DOWNGRADE to LOW (price + form factor + strategic drift toward delivery robots).
6) Funding/pivot: confirmed. €5M Jul-2024 + €5M Jan-2025 seed accurate. The €11M is a separate government GRANT for delivery robots (PABLO, 2025–2028), running in PARALLEL — glasses not abandoned (CES 2026 honoree, Arrow production scaling). Not a pivot away, but a strategic broadening.
7) status: "shipping" confirmed — but caveat: shipping EXCLUSIVELY in Romania (3–6 mo lead time), international by individual arrangement, broader market launch early 2026. Effectively early-commercial / Romania-only, not globally shipping.
8) CE certified confirmed; pursuing Class I medical + FDA per claim (plausible, not independently re-verified here).

name: biped.ai NOA
one_liner: Shoulder-worn AI 'copilot' with 170° obstacle detection, GPS nav, and scene description via spatialized bone-conduction audio.
funding: Founded 2021; Honda-incubated AI; funding amount not disclosed in this pass.
form_factor: Shoulder/chest-worn unit (~950 g) with 3 depth cameras; can be worn over a backpack; bone-conduction spatial audio; 6-8 h battery; weather resistant.
price_usd: ~$2,000–$2,950 (biped site quotes €1,850–€2,750 by model; community/AppleVis reports ~£3,000 ≈ $3,750 for top config)
target_users: Blind/low-vision adults who keep their cane or guide dog and want hands-free obstacle+nav augmentation.
key_features: 170° obstacle detection (head-height to drop-offs), GPS turn-by-turn, real-time AI scene description, object finding, claims 77% fewer false alerts. Complements cane/dog rather than replacing.
why:
HIGH threat and HIGH overlap — the closest real competitor to our mobility wedge, and the card's errors actually make it MORE threatening, not less.

Direct overlap: NOA's core job is exactly ours — real-time obstacle/drop-off/staircase detection via depth cameras + spatial bone-conduction audio ('hands-free, keep your cane'), plus GPS nav. It is a mature, shipping, multi-country product with a Honda-licensed perception stack and real O&M-trainer distribution — a credible incumbent, not vaporware.

Where the card was wrong erodes TWO of our four differentiators:
- 'Offline' is NOT our exclusive: NOA already does on-device obstacle detection offline and only sends cloud-AI features online. Our 'fully offline core' is parity here, not a moat. Our remaining edge is doing MORE offline (scene description on-device) and being truly cloud-optional.
- 'No subscription' is weaker as a wedge: NOA already offers a one-time, no-monthly-fee purchase. Our freemium-free-basics still wins on PRICE and on 'free,' but not on 'subscription-free per se.'

Where we still genuinely differentiate (real wedges to defend):
1) PRICE: our sub-$700 freemium vs NOA's ~$2,000–$3,750. ~3-5x cheaper is our strongest lever, especially given AppleVis users explicitly named price + bulk as NOA's two biggest, hardest-to-fix flaws.
2) FORM FACTOR: our ~30g glasses + neck-box vs NOA's ~1kg backpack-style vest. Reviewers call NOA bulky — our wearability is a direct counter to a named pain point.
3) FREE safety-grade basics for a population where paying is a barrier.
4) Self-learning cloud agent / medical (caregiver) expansion — orthogonal to NOA today, though their new free 'NOA Chat' WhatsApp assistant shows they're moving toward a software/freemium layer, so this lead is time-boxed.

Net: treat biped.ai NOA as the benchmark competitor. Compete on price + lightweight glasses form factor + free basics; do NOT lead marketing with 'offline' or 'no subscription' as if unique — NOA blunts both.
corrections:
The competitor is REAL and SHIPPING (Switzerland, sold in 20+ countries, EU+US market entry), but the card had four material errors:

1) PRICE understated ~2-3x. Card said "~$1,000 + ~$40/mo." biped's own site states the device costs €1,850–€2,750 depending on model (~$2,000–$2,950 USD); AppleVis community reports ~£3,000 (~$3,750) for the full config. The "$40/mo" was not corroborated for NOA and likely conflated with a competitor (Glide). 

2) OFFLINE wrong. Card said offline=no. biped's FAQ states obstacle detection/avoidance and navigation to SAVED destinations work standalone WITHOUT internet (on-device computer vision on 3x Intel RealSense D430 depth cameras); only cloud AI features (real-time scene description, OCR/text recognition) need connectivity. NOA is a HYBRID, not online-only — architecturally the same offline-core + optional cloud-enhanced model we use.

3) SUBSCRIPTION not mandatory. There are TWO plans: (a) one-time purchase with NO monthly fee, or (b) a hybrid FINANCING plan (e.g. Australia: 48 × AUD 179, after which it auto-ends and you keep the device). So 'subscription:yes' is wrong — it's optional financing, not a recurring service fee.

4) FORM FACTOR / WEIGHT off. It's a VEST/HARNESS worn like a backpack, weighing ~1+ kg (card said shoulder-worn ~950g — lighter than reality). Battery is ~3h per charge, ships with 2 batteries (~6h total). 170° FOV and bone-conduction spatial audio are confirmed correct.

FUNDING: Founded 2021 (Lausanne, Switzerland). Honda Research Institute Europe licensed its autonomous-driving risk-analysis tech to biped in 2022. Raised ~CHF 1.2M (round announced for US expansion / EU growth); investors include Serpentine Ventures, Juchum Foundation, angels. NOT 'undisclosed' — ~CHF 1.2M is the disclosed figure.

NEW SIGNAL: biped launched 'NOA Chat,' a free/freemium AI mobility assistant in WhatsApp (CHF 9.99/mo for unlimited) — evidence they are ALSO building a software/freemium layer, narrowing the gap with our differentiation.

name: Ara (Strap Tech)
one_liner: Chest-worn ultrasonic+lidar device that maps surroundings and conveys obstacle location via sequenced haptic vibrations (cane replacement).
funding: TIME Best Inventions 2022; selling in limited batches (250 units), funding undisclosed.
form_factor: Lightweight rechargeable chest-worn unit with ultrasonic + lidar sensors; haptic-only feedback.
target_users: Blind users seeking a white-cane replacement with multi-level (ground/chest/head) obstacle detection.
key_features: Continuous ultrasonic+lidar scanning, detects obstacles at head/chest/ground level, sequenced vibration feedback, claims up to 80% fewer hits / 30% lower injury risk. Haptic, no audio/scene AI.
why:
REAL, not a hallucination — confirmed shipping by company shop page, a Jan-2025 Easterseals hands-on review, TIME Best Inventions 2022, and Crunchbase. But low threat to us: (1) Different device class — Ara is a neck+waist haptic harness (ultrasonic+LiDAR) that ONLY does obstacle/collision detection via vibration patterns at top/mid/low height. No camera, no audio, no scene description, no OCR, no navigation, no AI software platform. It is a white-cane replacement, full stop. (2) Price gap — $2,750 vs our sub-$700, ~4x more expensive, and no freemium/free tier (one-time hardware purchase). Our free, no-subscription base directly undercuts them on the safety-affordability axis. (3) Tiny, old funding — only ~$1.3M pre-seed/seed (Apr 2021, Platform Capital); no large raise found since, sold in 250-unit batches → limited scale/runway, more of a boutique than a market-mover. (4) Their platform ceiling is low — haptic-only means they cannot extend into OCR/scene-description/healthcare-vitals the way our camera+VLM stack and modular sensing engine can. The ONLY overlap is the core mobility job-to-be-done (don't-hit-obstacles), where we compete on cheaper, audio-rich (bone-conduction "step ahead 1.5m"), camera-based detection vs their pricey haptic-only approach. Worth watching as a mobility-category reference point, but not a strategic threat to our offline + freemium + AI-platform positioning.</why_threat_or_not>
<parameter name="corrections">1) Funding NOT undisclosed — it's a disclosed ~$1.3M pre-seed/seed (Apr 2021), led by Platform Capital, with MassChallenge, Alamo Angels, International Accelerator. No major raise found since (old, small — runway flag). 2) Form factor: "chest-worn" is imprecise — it is a neck-and-waist HARNESS detecting obstacles at top/mid/low height levels (per Easterseals Jan-2025 review). 3) Discount framing: current public promo is code "Earlybird500" for -$500 (→$2,250); the "$600 downpayment" detail in the dossier is not the headline offer. Base price $2,750 confirmed. Sold in batches of 250 units (Batch 0 & 1 sold out, Batch 2 open). 4) Overlap downgraded high → low: haptic-only cane-replacement vs our camera+YOLO+ToF+bone-conduction+AI-software-platform; only the mobility JTBD overlaps. Everything else in the dossier (shipping, offline=yes, subscription=no, ultrasonic+LiDAR, haptic-only/no scene AI, ~80% fewer collisions / 30% lower injury claims, TIME 2022) is accurate.</corrections>

name: Glide (Glidance)
one_liner: World's-first autonomous self-guided rolling robot on a handle that physically steers blind users along mapped routes.
funding: Raised $1.5M (The Robot Report); preorders open with deposit scheme.
form_factor: Two-wheeled ground robot with telescoping handle (<8 lb); user nudges it forward and it steers; 6+ h battery, smart standby.
target_users: Blind/low-vision users wanting an autonomous guide alternative to cane/dog, in familiar and new environments.
key_features: 'Sensible Wayfinding' cloud CV service connecting all Glides for route mapping, target-of-interest ID, obstacle avoidance. Not wearable (handheld/rolling). Founder Amos Miller (ex-Microsoft, RP).
why:
REAL company, not a hallucination — confirmed against Glidance's own primary sources (product page, FAQ, funding announcement). Founder Amos Miller (ex-Microsoft, has retinitis pigmentosa). Status: in beta, shipping to earliest preorder cohorts (Pioneers/Founding Gliders) spring 2026; later cohorts early 2027; preorder program now CLOSED (waitlist only). Confirmed: $1,499 retail + $30/mo "Sensible Wayfinding" subscription; iOS/Android companion app required.

WHY ONLY LOW THREAT (different category, despite both targeting blind mobility):
1. Form factor is fundamentally different — Glide is an ~8 lb handheld two-wheeled GROUND ROBOT on a 25in telescoping handle that the user pushes and that physically steers them. We are lightweight (~30g frame) head-worn glasses + neck compute box. They replace the cane/dog with a wheeled device; we are wearable, hands-free-ish, and don't occupy a hand the same way. Buyers self-select on form factor.
2. Price/model wedge is opposite to ours — they are $1,499 + $30/mo mandatory subscription. We are sub-$700 with free, no-subscription offline base (a deliberate safety/affordability wedge for blind users). Our pricing directly undercuts their model.
3. Offline posture is comparable but not identical — their core obstacle avoidance / walk-straight / line-of-sight targeting works OFFLINE (so our 'they're cloud-only' framing in the original card was WRONG), but their full AI suite (Sensible Wayfinding route mapping, target-of-interest ID) REQUIRES connectivity + subscription. Our differentiation is full mobility-grade safety offline AND free — they gate the rich features behind cloud+pay.
4. Roadmap overlap is thin — their moat is the fleet-mapped 'Sensible Wayfinding' network for outdoor/indoor route guidance, a different bet than our modular on-device perception engine expanding into OCR/scene-description/healthcare-eyewear.

Net: well-funded, credible, shipping-soon, and the most serious dedicated-mobility competitor for blind users — but a hardware category apart (ground robot vs. wearable) and on the opposite pricing/subscription axis. They validate the market (good signal) more than they threaten our wedge.</why_threat_or_not>
<parameter name="overlap_with_us">low
corrections: 1. PRICE: original said '~$1,500 retail (preorder -40%)' — actual is $1,499 retail, preorder discount was 20% off MSRP (not 40%). +$20 US shipping. 2. FUNDING: original said 'Raised $1.5M' — that was only the (oversubscribed) PRE-SEED round (announced ~Jan 2025). Aggregators now show ~$7.21M total raised across later rounds (investors incl. Outlander VC, Break Trail Ventures, Kalamazoo Forward Ventures, UCeed, Spacestation). 3. OFFLINE: original said offline='no' — INCORRECT. Per their FAQ, core obstacle avoidance / walking straight / line-of-sight targeting work OFFLINE with no connectivity; only the full AI suite needs cloud. Correct value is 'partial'. 4. STATUS: 'pre-launch' still technically correct but should note it is in BETA with spring-2026 shipping to earliest cohorts; preorder program is now CLOSED (waitlist only). 5. WEIGHT: '<8 lb' — their product page states ~8 lb / two 7.5in wheels / 25in handle. 6. OVERLAP: original 'medium' overstated — corrected to 'low' (different hardware category + opposite pricing model). Threat lowered medium→low accordingly.

name: WeWALK Smart Cane 2
one_liner: AI smart cane with chest-height obstacle detection, turn-by-turn voice nav, and a Gemini-powered voice assistant.
funding: TDK/Microsoft/Google partnerships; featured CES 2025; funding undisclosed.
form_factor: Smart cane handle attaching to a standard cane; ultrasonic chest-height sensor; ~20 h battery; pairs with phone app.
price_usd: $850 one-time + $4.99/mo AI subscription, OR $1,150 one-time with AI included (confirmed). UK: ~£3.99/mo. Subscription tiers: Safety / Navigation / AI.
target_users: Cane users wanting added above-waist obstacle detection plus AI navigation/exploration.
key_features: Chest-height obstacle detection, turn-by-turn voice navigation, guided exploration of surroundings, Gemini cloud voice assistant. Still a cane (not hands-free), cloud-dependent.
why: Real, shipping product, not a hallucination — confirmed on the official wewalk.io store (multiple SKUs, in-cart checkout, installments) and corroborated by CES 2025 Engadget coverage and retailers (Amazon, LS&S, CNIB, Sterling). It is a genuine competitor in the blind-mobility space but the overlap is only MEDIUM and bounded for structural reasons: (1) Different form factor — it's still a white CANE (handle attached to a cane), not hands-free wearable glasses. We are head-mounted/hands-free; a user choosing glasses is not choosing a cane, and vice versa. They co-exist more than they substitute. (2) Sensing is shallow — single ultrasonic time-of-flight sensor for chest/head-height obstacles + haptic/audio. We use camera + on-device YOLO + ToF depth, which is a far richer perception stack (object classification, scene description, OCR roadmap) vs. their proximity-beep. (3) Cloud-dependent vs. our offline-first wedge — only dumb ground-detection works without a phone; their obstacle-detection, navigation, AND AI assistant are all behind paid cloud/subscription tiers. Our differentiator (complete safety-grade mobility free + fully offline, no subscription) is precisely the gap they leave open. They charge $4.99/mo for the very assistant layer; their base obstacle detection sits behind a 'Safety' add-on. (4) Not freemium-safe — gating mobility features behind subscription is exactly the positioning we argue is unsafe for blind users; this is a wedge we can attack in messaging. WHY NOT HIGH: they have real distribution (CNIB training partnership, Amazon, specialty AT retailers), an established brand from v1, and TDK sensor partnership — credible incumbent in adjacent category. WHY NOT LOW: same end-user (blind/low-vision), overlapping job-to-be-done (above-waist obstacle avoidance + voice nav), similar price band. Watch item, not an existential threat. Funding is modest (~$3.16M total, ~30 investors, NEC X-led Feb 2024) — they are a small startup, not a giant, so they can be out-engineered on the perception stack.
corrections: 1) ASSISTANT ENGINE: Card says 'Gemini-powered.' Primary CES 2025 coverage (Engadget) states the assistant is GPT-powered at launch, not Gemini. Some later marketing/aggregator copy says Gemini — WeWALK may have switched or markets it differently by region, but the 'Gemini-powered' claim is not solidly sourced; treat LLM backend as GPT-at-launch / unconfirmed-current. 2) FUNDING/PARTNERSHIPS: Card frames 'TDK/Microsoft/Google partnerships' as if funding. Reality: TDK is a SENSOR-HARDWARE partner (ultrasonic/motion/baro sensors), NOT a funder. WeWALK's actual funding is ~$3.16M total over ~6 rounds / ~30 investors, lead NEC X (Feb 2024), latest a small ~$0.9M round Nov 2025 — a small startup. TDK Ventures does NOT appear as an investor. Microsoft tie dates to v1 (Azure/AI for Accessibility era); Google link rests only on the unconfirmed Gemini claim. So 'funding undisclosed' should read 'small (~$3M total), sensor partnership with TDK ≠ funding.' 3) PRICE: card's '$850 (+$4.99/mo AI) or $1,150 one-time' is CORRECT and verified. 4) OFFLINE 'no' is essentially correct but nuance: basic ground/cane detection works without a phone; all smart features (obstacle, nav, AI) are cloud/subscription — better stated as 'mostly no / cloud-dependent for smart features.' 5) STATUS 'shipping' CONFIRMED (pre-orders opened at CES Jan 2025, now generally available via official store + Amazon + AT retailers).

name: NextVPU AngelEye (天使眼)
one_liner: Chinese 40g sunglasses-style aid (~$750) doing obstacle avoidance, navigation, step detection, reading and item-finding — closest match to our weight+price.
funding: NextVPU (肇观电子); Indiegogo crowdfunded; established CV chip/SoC company.
form_factor: Sunglasses-style frame reduced to ~40 g + wired controller; warning sounds for obstacles.
price_usd: ~$750 (original Indiegogo-era glasses; unverified at primary source — current glasses price not publicly listed; the 30g "Smart Reader" is a different OCR product)
target_users: Blind/low-vision users globally wanting an affordable glasses-form obstacle + reading aid.
key_features: Obstacle avoidance (auto-on), navigation, step/stairs notification, OCR reading, object finding. Claims 10,000+ users helped. Directly overlaps our form factor (glasses, ~40g) and price (~$750) — the single closest analog.
why:
REAL and well-funded — not a hallucination. NextVPU/肇观电子 is a genuine Shanghai computer-vision SoC company (founded 2016-05), with FCC-filed AngelEye controller hardware (fccid.io 2AOQI-ANGELEYEB01) and funding through Series D (B轮 ¥300M 2020, C轮 2022, D轮 ¥200M 2023, +D+/D++ 2023-24) — far past the "Indiegogo startup" framing. AngelEye is ONE product line of a chip company (N171 vision SoC), not its whole business.

On paper the overlap is high (glasses-form blind aid, obstacle avoidance + steps + OCR + object finding, China, ~$750 band). But three corrections lower the LIVE threat to medium:

1) NOT offline-first like us. Per the official user manual: obstacle avoidance, steps, and OCR run standalone on the controller, BUT "Navigation, positioning and software updates are only available when Internet is connecting." Their highest-value mobility function (navigation) is cloud-gated — exactly the seam our fully-offline wedge exploits. Mark offline = partial, not full.

2) The "~40g sunglasses" weight is a conflation. 30g/1.06oz is the AngelEye Smart READER (a clip-on OCR device, 1.5h battery) — a different product. The obstacle glasses are a stereo-camera frame + a separate wired controller box (FCC ANGELEYEB01) — a split-body design like ours, not a 40g monolith.

3) Status = pivoted/drifting, not cleanly shipping. The current angeleyeglobal.com homepage and product nav now lead EXCLUSIVELY with OCR readers (Wearable + Desktop Reader); the obstacle-avoidance glasses are no longer featured in the top nav (still on deep product URLs). Their commercial center of gravity has visibly moved mobility-glasses → reading hardware — the same "reading is where the revenue went" gravity well (OrCam/Envision) in our own thesis.

No subscription found anywhere; updates explicitly free (so subscription = no, correcting "unknown"). Net: a credible, capitalized, China-cost incumbent that already built the glasses we're building — but it is (a) cloud-dependent for navigation, (b) split-body not 40g, and (c) currently de-prioritizing mobility for reading. That makes it a benchmark and a cautionary tale more than an acute head-on threat. Watch for a relaunch of the glasses line.
corrections: (1) offline "partial" is correct but must be read as: avoidance/OCR offline, NAVIGATION is online-only — not a true offline-first competitor. (2) Weight "~40g" is wrong/conflated: 30g is the separate OCR Smart Reader; the obstacle glasses are a frame + separate wired controller box (split-body), not a 40g monolithic frame. (3) Status "shipping" → pivoted/drifting: official site now leads exclusively with OCR readers; obstacle glasses dropped from top product nav. (4) subscription "unknown" → no (manual says updates are free; no subscription found). (5) funding framing "Indiegogo crowdfunded startup" understates it — VC-backed CV-chip company funded through Series D (¥300M B-round 2020, ¥200M D-round 2023). (6) overlap_with_us high → medium and threat high → medium given the cloud-nav dependence + pivot away from mobility. (7) "10,000+ users" claim vs site's "100K+ customers / 36 countries" — vendor marketing, treat as unverified.

name: TAMI (Lighthouse Tech)
one_liner: Swiss fashion-frame glasses that detect waist-to-head obstacles to 3m and steer via temple vibrations — a sleek, haptic-only mobility frame.
funding: Founded 2020, Ticino; uses Acconeer radar sensors; funding undisclosed.
form_factor: Fashion eyewear frame, six style models; front vision/radar sensor; vibration receivers in the temples (haptic-only, no audio/AI scene).
price_usd: unknown (not published; no public price on site — "ORDER NOW" goes to contact/order flow; sold direct in CH/DE only)
target_users: Blind/vision-impaired users wanting a discreet, fashionable obstacle-detection frame.
key_features: Detects all objects waist-to-head height up to 3m; directional temple vibration (stronger/weaker steers you); no obstacle = no vibration. Launched Swiss/German markets May-2025. Closest 'sleek glasses' competitor — but haptic-only, no scene/reading.
why:
REAL and SHIPPING — not a hallucination. Confirmed via primary sources: official site has a live "ORDER NOW" button; CE certification obtained 2025-06-30; first sales of TAMI commenced 2025-07-30 (Venture Kick profile). Company is genuine: Lighthouse Tech SA, incorporated 2020-11-17, Morbio Inferiore/Ticino, Switzerland. Uses Acconeer A121 60GHz radar (NOT generic "radar sensors" — and it's radar, not camera/ToF). Fully offline/standalone confirmed (push-button operation, no app/cloud/account; user guide mentions only low-power Bluetooth with no pairing/app flow — likely internal/future). No subscription, one-time purchase + 2yr EU warranty.

WHY LOW THREAT despite "high/high" in the input — the original assessment overstated overlap:
1. DIFFERENT CATEGORY OF DEVICE. TAMI is haptic-ONLY (temple vibrations, left/right/bilateral, amplitude = proximity). It does NOT do audio, scene description, OCR/reading, object recognition, indoor nav, or any AI software. It is a single-purpose obstacle-warning frame for the upper-body/head zone the cane misses. We are a camera + on-device YOLO + ToF + bone-conduction voice MOBILITY device that is also a software PLATFORM (OCR, scene description, find-my-object, traffic lights, future cloud agent). Our roadmap (platform, freemium cloud, healthcare expansion) has essentially zero counterpart on their side.
2. DIFFERENT SENSING. Radar (range/presence only, no semantics) vs our camera+YOLO+ToF (semantic classification + precise depth: "step ahead 1.5m"). TAMI can say "something is on your left," not what it is. This is a meaningfully weaker mobility signal than ours.
3. DIFFERENT MARKET/GEOGRAPHY. Sold only in Switzerland & Germany (launched May 2025, Sight City Frankfurt), direct/contact-based, no public US price, no online checkout. We target sub-$700 freemium globally. No US overlap today.
4. EARLY-STAGE / UNDER-CAPITALIZED for a hardware co. Funding is grant-heavy and small: Venture Kick CHF 150k (2024); seed CHF 500k closed May 2024 (a CHF 1.5M round was still being raised in 2025 with ~CHF 600k committed); Innosuisse CHF 1.2M (Nov 2025, with ETH Zurich + CSEM); plus Toyota Mobility Foundation grants. Total disclosed well under ~CHF 2.5M — a small Swiss medtech startup, not a giant.

NET: TAMI validates the "discreet fashion-frame mobility aid" thesis and is the closest 'sleek glasses' form-factor competitor, but it is a narrow haptic-only radar accessory in a different region with no AI/software platform, no audio, and no healthcare path. It is more a stigma-friendly cane-complement than a competitor to a camera-AI assistive platform. Watch it (form-factor benchmark, weight target — theirs is ~55g monolithic vs our ~30g split design), but it is LOW direct threat. Overlap = low.

why: CONFIRMED REAL and the most direct competitor on this list. (2) They are SHIPPING to real users in US/Canada today while we are pre-board-bringup; they have a head start on reviews and word-of-mouth in a small, tight-knit community. (3) Their freemium framing (free Explorer tier + $9.99/mo Independence) directly blunts our "free, no-subscription" wedge — we can't claim freemium novelty, only that OUR base tier is FULLY offline+free whereas theirs is cloud-gated even on the free tier. They have NO real-time obstacle avoidance / ToF depth / haptic 'step ahead' — our core wedge. name: Microsoft Seeing AI
one_liner: FREE giant-backed phone app reading text, describing scenes/photos, scanning products — the commodity that helped kill paid reading hardware.

why:
Real, free, no-subscription, giant-backed, actively shipped on iOS + Android — exactly what it claims to be. HIGH threat, but NOT as a head-to-head product rival. Overlap is MEDIUM and entirely on the READING / information-access axis (OCR, scene/photo description, product/barcode scan, GenAI descriptions, doc-chat) — phone-in-hand, not real-time walking. Its real danger is strategic: as a free, no-account, Microsoft-distributed app it sets the FREE COMMODITY FLOOR for visual information access. So any plan to monetize reading/scene-description/OCR is dead on arrival. This validates rather than threatens our thesis: it confirms our wedge MUST be MOBILITY (real-time obstacle/path via on-device YOLO + ToF + bone conduction), which Seeing AI explicitly does NOT do — it is phone-in-hand and cloud-dependent, so it cannot deliver low-latency, always-on, hands-free, offline mobility safety. Where it pressures us: (a) it commoditizes our future cloud-enhanced OCR/scene-description/find-things premium features, so those can't be our paid moat; (b) it proves blind users adopt free tools from big brands, raising the bar on our free-basic-tier promise; (c) if Microsoft ever pairs it with an always-on wearable camera and adds offline mobility, threat escalates sharply — worth monitoring. Net: a high-gravity reference point that disciplines our positioning (offline + mobility-safety-grade + hands-free wearable), not a direct competitor for our core wedge.</parameter>

corrections: No material errors in the input claim — every field checked out against primary sources. Refinements: (1) "offline: partial" is accurate but should read as "mostly cloud, thin offline floor." Only basic channels work without internet (short-text OCR, currency, color, light/brightness); ALL high-value modern features — scene description, GenAI rich photo descriptions, handwriting recognition, document chat — require internet. So offline is a fallback floor, not a real differentiator against our fully-offline pitch. (2) Funding "Microsoft." is trivially true but worth stating plainly: it is a Microsoft first-party app and "an ongoing research project," not a venture-funded startup — no funding/runway dynamic to track; it persists as long as Microsoft wants. (3) Last shipped update v5.6.1 on 2025-09-04 (Apple App Store), confirming active maintenance, not abandonment. (4) Android launch (Dec 2023) with GenAI rich descriptions + doc-chat confirmed via Microsoft Accessibility Blog — input feature claims correct.

name: Be My Eyes / Be My AI
one_liner: FREE GPT-4-powered describe-anything app + live volunteers, now hands-free on Meta Ray-Bans — the de-facto AI-access standard for the blind.
funding: Backed by OpenAI partnership; Meta Ray-Ban integration.
form_factor: iOS/Android app; ALSO the first/only accessibility app on Meta Ray-Ban AI glasses (volunteer calls, hands-free).
target_users: All blind/low-vision users for AI image Q&A and live human help; now glasses-wearers via Meta.
key_features: GPT-4 conversational scene/photo descriptions (185 languages) + volunteer video calls, always free. On Meta Ray-Bans: hands-free volunteer calls through the glasses' camera+speakers. STRATEGIC: this is the channel by which a GIANT (Meta) reaches blind users — the platform threat to watch.
why:
REAL and shipping — confirmed against primary sources. Be My AI (GPT-4-with-Vision describe-anything, 185+ languages) plus live volunteer video calls; the app is genuinely free with no subscription for blind/low-vision users (freemium funded by enterprise: "Be My Eyes for Work", brand-partner customer service). The Meta Ray-Ban integration is real and EXPANDED on 2026-03-11 at CSUN: hands-free "Hey Meta, Be My Eyes" volunteer calls, now also routing to trusted contacts and to brand support teams (Tesco, Sony, Hilton, Amtrak, Clearblue, Zain). This is the de-facto AI-access standard for the blind and the channel through which a GIANT (Meta) reaches our exact users — so threat_level stays HIGH as a platform/category-definition and distribution threat.

BUT direct product overlap with us is LOW, not medium. Be My Eyes is a fundamentally different category: it is CLOUD/ONLINE-ONLY (GPT-4 vision + remote human volunteers both require connectivity) and it is a DESCRIBE-ANYTHING + human-help access tool. It does NOT do our wedge — real-time, on-device, safety-grade mobility/obstacle avoidance (YOLO + ToF depth + bone-conduction "step ahead 1.5m"). A volunteer call or a cloud round-trip is unusable for split-second walking-safety feedback, and it doesn't work where there's no signal. Our offline + mobility-safety-grade + no-cloud-latency positioning is exactly the gap they structurally cannot fill on Meta's consumer hardware. Where we DO collide longer-term is the cloud-enhancement layer (OCR/scene description / "what's in front of me") — there Be My AI is the incumbent free benchmark and sets user price expectations at $0, which pressures any paid cloud tier we add.

Net: not a head-to-head mobility competitor (overlap LOW), but a HIGH strategic threat because it owns the blind-user mindshare + the Meta glasses distribution channel and anchors "AI vision help = free." Differentiate hard on offline mobility safety; do NOT try to out-describe Be My AI.
corrections:
1) FUNDING — card said "Backed by OpenAI partnership; Meta Ray-Ban integration." That conflates partnerships with funding. ACTUAL: VC-backed startup, $6.1M Series A+ on 2025-01-22 led by Enable Ventures (with ECMC Educational Impact Fund, National Federation of the Blind, LiteCap, Samaritan Partners); ~$17.4M total raised. OpenAI and Meta are technology/product PARTNERS, not investors. 2024: 361% revenue growth, 76% MAU growth, 43M+ user requests/yr.
2) FORM FACTOR — card said Be My Eyes is "the first/only accessibility app on Meta Ray-Ban." Updated/precise: per Be My Eyes' own pages it is the "first and only accessibility tech for blind/low-vision on Meta AI Glasses," now across Ray-Ban Meta AND Oakley Meta. First glasses release was volunteer calls only; AI features and contact/brand-support calling added/expanded through early 2026.
3) OVERLAP — card rated overlap_with_us "medium"; corrected DOWN to "low" (different category: cloud describe-anything + human help, NOT on-device mobility safety). Threat_level "high" retained but reframed as a platform/distribution/price-anchoring threat, not a product-feature competitor.
4) OFFLINE confirmed "no" (cloud GPT-4 + remote volunteers both require connectivity); SUBSCRIPTION confirmed "no" for end users (freemium; paid tiers are enterprise-side); STATUS confirmed "shipping." Nothing refuted — all card claims check out; only the above nuances corrected.

name: Meta Ray-Ban AI Glasses (accessibility)
one_liner: The GIANT in the room: mainstream $300-400 camera glasses + open-ear AI now hosting Be My Eyes — could absorb 'access' use-cases at consumer scale.
form_factor: Mainstream Ray-Ban frames with camera + open-ear speakers + Meta AI; Gen 1/Gen 2.
price_usd: $299 entry (Ray-Ban/Oakley Meta Gen 2); $499 Oakley Vanguard; ~$800 Meta Ray-Ban Display. Accessibility features (Meta AI describe + Be My Eyes) are free, no separate fee.
target_users: Mass consumers; increasingly blind/low-vision via Be My Eyes + Meta AI describe features.
key_features: Hands-free Meta AI scene description + Be My Eyes volunteer calls through the glasses. NOT mobility/obstacle-safety (no depth/ToF, cloud-dependent, no real-time path guidance) — which is exactly the gap we exploit. But strategically this is the giant to NOT fight head-on on 'access/description'; our defensibility must be offline mobility-safety they won't prioritize.
why:
REAL and SHIPPING — confirmed against multiple primary sources, not a hallucination. Meta + EssilorLuxottica consumer smart glasses with camera + open-ear audio + Meta AI. Two accessibility capabilities directly confirmed on meta.com/ai-glasses/accessibility and meta.com/ai-glasses/blind-visually-impaired: (1) "Hey Meta, describe what I'm seeing" scene description; (2) hands-free Be My Eyes — and as of an EXPANDED partnership announced 2026-03-11 at CSUN, blind users can now also reach trained brand service reps (Tesco, Sony, Amtrak, Hilton, etc.) hands-free across 21 markets, free to the user. So this is not a stale 2024 claim — Meta is ACTIVELY investing in the blind/low-vision segment in 2026.

The submitted card's claims hold up under scrutiny:
- Price ~$300-400: confirmed; entry is now $299. (Slight correction: the premium tier expanded — Display model is ~$800, Vanguard $499.)
- Subscription "no": confirmed — accessibility features are free, no subscription.
- "Offline: no": CONFIRMED and this is the crux. Meta's own help docs state an internet connection is REQUIRED for Meta AI; only photo/video capture and a handful of legacy-language translations work offline. Scene description and Be My Eyes are cloud/network-dependent. This is exactly our wedge.
- "NOT mobility/obstacle-safety": CONFIRMED — the blind/low-vision page describes ONLY scene description, text reading, object ID, and remote human/AI assistance. No depth sensing, no ToF, no real-time obstacle/path/step guidance, no walking-safety latency guarantee. They are not building this.

WHY HIGH THREAT despite only medium feature-overlap: this is a platform/category threat, not a feature competitor. Meta has effectively unlimited capital, consumer-scale distribution (EssilorLuxottica retail + $299 price), and is the default mainstream device a newly-blind user or their family reaches for. They own "access/description" use-cases at scale. If we ever position Second Eye primarily as "describe the scene / read text / call a volunteer," we lose head-on. Strategy implication is UNCHANGED by this verification: do NOT fight on access/description; our defensibility is (a) offline mobility-safety-grade real-time obstacle avoidance (depth/ToF + on-device YOLO + bone-conduction, works with zero connectivity — safety-critical for a blind person who cannot depend on cloud latency or signal) and (b) freemium-free complete-offline baseline. Meta structurally will not prioritize sub-second offline obstacle guarantees on a fashion-first consumer frame with no depth sensor — that gap is genuine and durable.

Overlap rated medium (not high): real overlap exists on scene-description / OCR / "describe surroundings" — which sits in our future software-platform roadmap, not our mobility wedge. So they overlap our PLATFORM ambitions but not our WEDGE. Keep the wedge narrow and offline.
corrections: Card was accurate; minor refinements: (1) Entry price is now $299 (not just "$300-400"); product line has expanded UPWARD with Meta Ray-Ban Display (~$800) and Oakley Vanguard ($499). (2) Be My Eyes integration EXPANDED on 2026-03-11 (CSUN) beyond volunteer calls to also include hands-free connection to brand customer-service reps in 21 markets — i.e. Meta is increasing, not decreasing, its accessibility investment. (3) "Offline: no" is correct but worth stating precisely: photo/video capture and six legacy-language translations work offline; ALL AI features (incl. scene description + Be My Eyes) require internet. Our offline-mobility differentiation is intact and verified.

name: Google Lookout
one_liner: FREE Google Android app (CV + GenAI) for text/explore/find/currency/food/image Q&A — another giant-backed commodity on the access layer.
form_factor: Android-only app, self-voicing, 30+ languages.
target_users: Android blind/low-vision users for reading and surroundings exploration.
key_features: Text, documents, explore (objects/people/text), currency, food labels, find objects, image Q&A. Free, no IAP. Android-only, phone-in-hand. Reinforces the free commodity access layer (with Seeing AI and Be My AI).
why:
真实存在且在售，但被原条目高估为 high threat — 实际重叠度低、威胁低。核实结论：

真实性：CONFIRMED。Google 官方产品，Android 应用 (包名 com.google.android.apps.accessibility.reveal)，由 Google 发布，官方 support 页 + Google 官方 blog 多处佐证。非幻觉。

状态：SHIPPING。Play Store 最近更新 2025-08-28，活跃维护中。当前 7 个模式：Text、Documents、Explore (beta)、Currency、Food labels、Images、Find（与条目列举基本一致）。

价格/订阅：免费，无内购、无订阅。CONFIRMED（与条目一致）。

离线：PARTIAL，但条目暗示的"足够离线"被夸大。官方文档只明确两类本地处理：(1) 文档/文本 OCR 在设备端、(2) Food labels 下载数据后可离线。其余核心模式——Explore、Images / Image Q&A、场景描述类——依赖云端、需联网。即 Lookout 的"看世界 / 问答"能力本质是 phone-in-hand + 云。

与我们的真实重叠 = LOW（条目标的 medium 偏高）：
- 形态错位：Lookout = 手机举着用的 app，自带语音；我们 = 可穿戴分体式眼镜（轻框 + 颈挂盒），免手持。盲人移动场景手持手机正是痛点，我们正面绕开。
- Wedge 错位：这是关键。Lookout 完全没有我们的核心 wedge——实时避障/mobility-safety（端侧 YOLO + ToF 深度 + 骨传导"前方1.5m台阶"）。它是"reading / explore / 识物 / 问答"访问层工具，不解决"安全行走"。两者解决的是不同 job。
- 离线错位：我们主打基础完整离线、安全保障级；Lookout 关键能力需云端联网。

为什么 threat = LOW 而非 high：它是巨头背书的免费访问层 commodity（与 Seeing AI、Be My AI 同层），会持续压低"识物/读字/问答"这类功能的付费意愿——这是真实的市场背景压力，需在定价/叙事上承认。但它不与我们的 mobility-safety wedge 直接竞争，不是可穿戴，不做实时避障。它压的是我们"未来软件层"(OCR/场景描述/找东西) 的潜在变现，而非我们赖以立足的护城河。结论：作为"免费访问层基线"它值得在竞品图里列出并比对，但作为对我们核心 wedge 的直接威胁应降级为 LOW。
corrections:
原条目 3 处需修正：
1. threat_level: "high" → 应为 LOW。Lookout 是手机 app 访问层工具，无 mobility/避障能力，不与我们核心 wedge 竞争，也非可穿戴。原"high"高估。
2. overlap_with_us: "medium" → 应为 LOW。除了"都属视障辅助"这一大类，功能/形态/wedge 三层均不重叠。
3. offline: "partial" 标注本身正确，但 one_liner/描述暗示其为"通用工具"且未点明——实际只有 OCR(设备端) 与 Food labels(下载后) 离线，Explore/Images/Image Q&A 等核心识物问答需联网云端。应明确："仅 OCR 与食品标签离线，识物/问答/场景描述依赖云。"
其余字段（free、no subscription、giant=Google、Android-only、shipping）均核实无误。

name: Aira (Visual Interpreting)
one_liner: Human-agent visual-interpreting subscription ($26-65+/mo) plus free Access AI — solves description via people, not autonomy; complements rather than replaces.
funding: Established; ~$12M earlier round; NFB partnership plans.
form_factor: Phone app + live trained human interpreter (and free 'Access AI' feature); used on phones and on glasses (e.g. via EchoVision).
price_usd: $26-$65+/mo (5 free min/48h; free at Access Partner sites)
target_users: Blind/low-vision users wanting a trained human to describe/read/navigate in real time.
key_features: Live human visual interpreters describe, read, navigate; free Access AI; free at thousands of partner locations. Needs a person + connectivity; recurring cost. Different model (human service) but competes for the 'help me right now' job and is a partner channel for glasses makers.

name: Lazarillo
one_liner: FREE accessible GPS app announcing streets, intersections, transit and POIs as you walk — the free wayfinding layer (no obstacle detection).
funding: Established accessibility-GPS company (B2B indoor mapping revenue).
form_factor: iOS/Android app, audio guidance.
target_users: Blind/low-vision pedestrians wanting accessible turn-by-turn + ambient POI announcements.
key_features: Audio routes, announces street, intersections, businesses, transit stops as you move; indoor accessible mapping for venues. Wayfinding only — no real-time obstacle/path safety (the job we own). Free.

name: Eyecane (CES 2025)
one_liner: Smart glasses for macular-degeneration (AMD) that relocate live 4K video into the user's viewable peripheral field — low-vision remap, not blind mobility.
form_factor: Smart glasses with 4K camera + mobile app; real-time video remap to peripheral vision.
target_users: AMD / central-vision-loss users with residual peripheral sight — NOT the totally blind.
key_features: Captures and re-displays live images from the impaired (central) area into the viewable peripheral area. Display-based low-vision aid; different user/job than our blind-mobility wedge. Listed for completeness of the 'glasses for vision loss' field.

name: Tongxing / 瞳行科技 (Hangzhou) AI助盲眼镜
one_liner: China's first AI blind-assist glasses — 45g, sub-3000 yuan (~$420), 300ms real-time road/obstacle voice reporting; closest cheap mobility-glasses rival, but Qwen-cloud + phone-tethered.
form_factor: Lightweight glasses (45g) + dedicated smartphone (provides power + network via cable) + Bluetooth ring + cane; 121° ultra-wide dual camera
target_users: China's ~17M visually impaired
key_features: Real-time obstacle/road-condition voice reporting every step at 300ms latency; bus-sign and road-sign recognition; environment overview; local (on-device) text recognition for storefronts and price tags; powered by Alibaba Qwen LLM (claimed 70% R&D cost cut). Launch Q1 2026. Next goal: fine motor tasks ('picking up food').
funding: Zhejiang University alumni team; backed by Alibaba Qwen integration; funding undisclosed

name: .lumen Glasses
one_liner: Romanian fully on-device 'self-driving' guide-dog-replacement headset — the ONLY truly-offline mobility glasses, but €9,999 (~15-20x us) and bulky.
form_factor: Bulky head-worn headset; 6 cameras + 2 IR laser projectors + 3 IMUs + GPS; haptic guidance; all compute on-device
target_users: Blind / severely visually impaired wanting guide-dog-level autonomous navigation
key_features: Fully offline on-device processing (no internet/extra equipment); detects safe walking paths, ground-level AND overhead obstacles, stairs, doors, bus stops, puddles, snow; haptic real-time guidance; works in dark via IR. Public debut CES 2026; sales Romania early 2026, US planned; targeted 10k units by end-2026.
funding: Romanian deep-tech startup; EIC/grant-backed; raised multi-million (undisclosed exact)

name: biped NOA (biped.ai)
one_liner: Chest-worn 'AI copilot' with RealSense depth — obstacle detection runs locally/offline, but it's a vest not glasses, needs phone for GPS/AI, subscription.
form_factor: Chest-worn vest/harness; Intel RealSense D430 depth module; processes images locally to cut latency
price_usd: ~$3,000 device (£2,399 start, EU €1,850–€2,750 by model). Alt plan: upfront + ~48×179 AUD/mo (~$120/mo) financing. Reported AI subscription ~$40/mo. NOT the ~$1,200 in the claim.
target_users: Blind/low-vision; complements (not replaces) cane or guide dog
key_features: 170° obstacle detection up to 10m day/night, centimeter-accurate depth; precision GPS turn-by-turn (Honda Research Institute self-driving software); VLM scene descriptions; object finding. Obstacle avoidance + navigation to SAVED destinations work without app; phone/network needed only to set new GPS destination or for AI scene features.
funding: Swiss startup (biped.ai); co-developed with Honda Research Institute; venture-backed
why: HIGH threat, HIGH overlap, but it attacks a different price tier and form factor. Shared DNA: same buyer (blind/low-vision), same mobility wedge (real-time obstacle avoidance via depth sensing), same bone-conduction output, same offline-core / cloud-optional split, same platform play (mobility -> scene description -> object finding -> indoor nav). It is a venture-backed, shipping, Honda-partnered incumbent with real users and O&M-trainer distribution — the most direct competitor to Second Eye's mobility wedge. WHERE WE WIN / DIFFERENTIATE: (1) PRICE — NOA is ~$3,000 and our wedge is sub-$700, so we undercut by ~4x; their device price (not subscription) is the real barrier for blind users on fixed incomes. (2) FORM FACTOR — NOA is a bulky chest/backpack vest with 3 RealSense cameras + a compute+battery box; our ~30g glasses + neck-box is far more discreet and socially acceptable (a top adoption driver in this category). (3) FREE / NO-SUBSCRIPTION SAFETY BASE — our pitch is that mobility-safety must never be paywalled; NOA gates its best AI behind ~$40/mo and high upfront cost. WHERE THEY'RE AHEAD: shipping product, real funding, Honda IP, GPS turn-by-turn (Honda self-driving software), indoor last-50-feet, multi-country distribution via O&M trainers, and a 75+ install base generating real-world data. Net: validates the market and the wedge, but our defensible angle is cheaper + lighter (glasses) + safety-base-free. Watch them closely; do not claim "offline" as our unique edge since NOA already runs obstacle detection locally.
corrections: PRICE is the big error: claim says "~£800 / ~$1,200 + ~$30-40/mo." Real current price is £2,399 start / EU €1,850–€2,750 depending on model (~$3,000), roughly 2.5x the claimed figure. The "~$40/mo" subscription figure is roughly corroborated by a user forum, but biped also sells a one-time-purchase plan with no monthly fee, so subscription is OPTIONAL not mandatory ("yes" in the claim is too strong). Depth sensor confirmed: three Intel RealSense D430 modules (claim said D430 — correct). Form factor: confirmed a vest/harness worn like a backpack, NOT glasses — claim correct. Funding confirmed: CHF 1.2M (Serpentine Ventures, SICTIC, Juchum Foundation, angels) for US entry + EU growth; Honda Research Institute licensed its ADAS/self-driving risk-analysis tech — claim correct. Status: actively shipping (75 units to 10+ countries incl. US as of 2024; new batches + 2025/2026 software updates "Live AI," 10 saved destinations). Founded 2021, Lausanne CH. Two unflagged details worth noting: NOA uses BONE-CONDUCTION audio (same output channel as Second Eye), and it explicitly positions as a COMPLEMENT to cane/dog, not a replacement.

name: Meta Ray-Ban AI Glasses + Be My Eyes
one_liner: The GIANT threat: cheap stylish glasses with 'Look and Tell' AI + free Be My Eyes — owns reading/scene at scale, but cloud-dependent and NOT mobility/obstacle-safety.
category: Mainstream AI glasses (reading/scene via accessibility apps)
form_factor: Standard Ray-Ban glasses form (~50g), camera + open-ear speakers; all AI in cloud/phone
price_usd: $299 (new own-brand Meta Glasses, June 2026) / $379 Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2 / $499 Oakley Meta / $799 Ray-Ban Display. Be My Eyes app = free.
target_users: Mainstream consumers; increasingly adopted by blind/low-vision via Be My Eyes
key_features: 'Look and Tell' AI scene Q&A, reads text/menus/signs, describes surroundings; double-tap to call a Be My Eyes volunteer hands-free (first/only accessibility tech on Meta glasses). Strong on reading/scene/information access. NOT designed for real-time obstacle/path mobility safety; fully cloud-dependent.
funding: Meta (+ EssilorLuxottica); Be My Eyes venture-backed
why:
Real and shipping at massive scale — confirmed by primary sources. Meta controls ~80% of the AI glasses market (Counterpoint). The Be My Eyes "Call a Volunteer" integration, which was "coming soon" in Aug 2025, is now LIVE (per bemyeyes.com) across US/UK/CA/AU/FR/DE/IN/ES and more — hands-free "Hey Meta, Be My Eyes" connects a blind user to a sighted volunteer through the glasses camera. Be My Eyes is free and unlimited to blind users forever (enterprise-funded "purpose and profit" model; $17.4M raised, Enable Ventures et al.) — so this is genuinely free + no-subscription assistive access at consumer scale, the most dangerous part of the threat.

WHY HIGH THREAT: it owns the reading/scene/information-access job (menus, signs, mail, "what am I looking at") for the blind community at a price/brand/distribution we cannot match, and it normalizes "AI glasses for blind people" in the public mind — it will be the default thing people recommend.

WHY OVERLAP IS ONLY MEDIUM (our wedge survives): the claim's core differentiator holds up under scrutiny. (1) NOT MOBILITY — Be My Eyes itself states the first release is "hands-free access to volunteer calls" only; nothing does real-time obstacle/path/step detection. There is no on-device YOLO+ToF depth, no bone-conduction "step ahead 1.5m." It cannot be a mobility-safety device. (2) NOT OFFLINE — Meta's own help docs confirm Meta AI ("Look and Tell") and Be My Eyes calls both REQUIRE Wi-Fi/data; only photo/video capture works offline. A dead zone or dead phone = no help. That is exactly the safety gap our fully-offline mobility core targets. (3) Volunteer-call model has human-in-the-loop latency and availability — fine for "read this label," useless for "don't step into traffic in the next 0.5s."

CORRECTIONS to the dossier: (a) price is now LOWER than stated — Meta launched own-brand "Meta Glasses" at $299 on 2026-06-23 (Gen 2 Ray-Ban is $379, not $300-380); update entry price to $299. (b) Integration status should read "live/shipping" not "first/only + coming soon" — it shipped. (c) Form factor ~50g all-in-glasses (no neck unit) is correct and is actually a point AGAINST us on comfort/style — our 30g frame + neck-box split must justify itself on the compute/battery the mobility engine needs, which Meta's form cannot host offline. Net: keep them as the #1 reference competitor for reading/scene, but do NOT let them define our category — our defensible wedge is offline mobility-safety, which they structurally cannot serve.
corrections: 1) PRICE: dossier says ~$300-380 for Gen 2; actual Gen 2 = $379, and Meta just launched cheaper own-brand "Meta Glasses" at $299 (2026-06-23) — entry price is now $299, lower than stated. 2) STATUS of Be My Eyes integration: dossier/sources frame it as new/"coming soon" (2025); it is now LIVE and shipping in US/UK/CA/AU/FR/DE/IN/ES+. 3) "first/only accessibility tech on Meta glasses" was true at announcement but is now an established shipping feature. Everything else (no subscription, cloud-dependent, not mobility/obstacle, Be My Eyes free + venture/enterprise-backed) is accurate.

name: Envision Glasses
one_liner: Google-Glass-based reading/scene glasses with SOME on-device offline features — but $1,899-2,499, no obstacle mobility, company struggling.
form_factor: Glasses on Google Glass Enterprise Edition 2 base; monocular display arm
target_users: Blind and low-vision
key_features: Strong OCR (60+ languages incl. handwriting), Instant/Scan/Batch Text, scene description, face/color/light/cash detection, find people/objects, Call Aira/Companion. On-device offline text reading (v2.2.0) + offline Envision Library export. No obstacle detection or mobility at all.

name: ARxVision (ARx AI Gen1.5)
one_liner: $599 head-worn 'talking camera' for the blind — but tethered to an Android phone for all AI, reading/scene only, no on-device obstacle detection.
form_factor: Head-worn headset/camera (4K/8MP) tethered via USB-C to an Android phone (Qualcomm recommended); enhanced audio
target_users: Blind and low-vision
key_features: Talking camera that pipes 4K visuals into Android AI apps; integrates Microsoft Seeing AI + NaviLens. Reading/scene/navigation-tag focus; all processing on the connected phone (not on-device, not standalone). No dedicated obstacle/depth mobility.

name: Strap Tech — Ara
one_liner: $2,750 chest-worn autonomous-vehicle-style sensor pod (radar/lidar/ultrasonic) for obstacle detection — mobility overlap, but pricey, not glasses, on-device status unclear.
form_factor: Chest-worn pod; AV-style sensor array (radar, lidar, ultrasonic); haptic feedback language
target_users: Blind/low-vision wanting a white-cane replacement
key_features: Detects obstacles at head/chest/below — bumps, holes, overhanging objects, steps; claims up to 80% fewer hits; haptic notifications learnable in <2 min. Sensor-fusion (not camera-AI-first) approach; offline/on-device behavior not clearly documented. Early-stage.
why: LOW threat, and the deep-dive reinforces it. Overlap is real but partial: Ara competes on the SAME mobility/obstacle-avoidance wedge and is genuinely OFFLINE — so it neutralizes our "offline" differentiator on that one feature. BUT it loses on every other axis vs. our positioning: (a) Price — $2,750 vs our sub-$700 target; ~4x more expensive, plus it gates buyers behind a $600 downpayment and multi-year financing. (b) Pricing model — pay-for-hardware-in-full / financed, NO freemium, NO free basic tier; our free-offline-safety-base is a structural wedge they don't have. (c) Architecture — sensor-fusion only (ultrasonic+lidar), NO camera + on-device YOLO. That caps them at "obstacle there / not there" with no semantics: a documented real user reported launch units were "extremely buggy," couldn't replace a cane, had no reliable distance measurement, and no way to distinguish obstacle size. Our camera-AI stack is what unlocks the platform roadmap (OCR, scene description, traffic-light/sign reading, find-my-object, indoor nav) — Ara's sensor-only design has no path to that. (d) Form factor — a multi-strap body harness vs our 30g glasses + neck box; ours is far less stigmatizing/wearable. (e) Capitalization — only ~$1.3M seed (2021), no visible Series A, tiny batches (250 units), 6+ years in and still early/buggy; weak ability to out-execute. NET: Ara validates that the offline mobility category is real and people will wear a body device, but it is a pricey, sensor-limited, no-free-tier, thinly-funded incumbent — not a platform competitor. Watch it as category proof, not as a kill-threat. (Note: a same-name "Ara" health smartwatch exists — ignore; name collision, not this company.)
corrections: 1) SENSORS: input says "radar, lidar, ultrasonic" — Strap's own site lists only ULTRASONIC + LIDAR. No radar. Remove radar. Approach is sensor-fusion (not camera-AI), confirmed. 2) OFFLINE: input "unknown" — CONFIRMED offline. Strap FAQ: standalone device, no smartphone/internet needed for core obstacle detection; internet only for firmware updates (via WPS WiFi). 3) SUBSCRIPTION: input "unknown" — no recurring SOFTWARE subscription. What exists is hardware FINANCING (installment plans $283/6mo down to $58/36mo, $600 downpayment). Mark optional/financing, not a true subscription. 4) FORM FACTOR: not a single "chest-worn pod" — it's an adjustable multi-strap HARNESS with a haptic actuator per strap (top/mid/low body coverage). 5) FUNDING/REGION: founder Diego Roel (Mexican), but company is Austin, TX (US) based, founded 2018. Funding is tiny: ~$1.3M seed (Apr 2021, Platform Capital) — no documented Series A. "Venture-backed" is technically true but materially under-capitalized. 6) PRICE: $2,750 confirmed accurate.

name: Glidance — Glide
one_liner: $1,499 + $30/mo self-guiding wheeled robot handle that detects obstacles incl. overhead — mobility overlap, but a rolling handheld (not glasses) and subscription-required.
form_factor: Handheld self-guided wheeled robot ('Sensible Wayfinding'); sensor array; not wearable
price_usd: $1,199 device-only (limited-time, 20% off ~$1,499 MSRP); bundles $1,500 (device + 1yr sub) / $2,350 (device + 4yr sub); + $30/month mandatory subscription
target_users: Blind and low-vision wanting autonomous wayfinding
key_features: Maps best routes, identifies targets of interest, avoids stationary + moving obstacles including overhead (branches, signs). Subscription required for the navigation AI/OTA updates; cloud-dependent. Demo days through 2025; not yet shipping.
why:
REAL company, confirmed via primary sources (glidance.io product/preorder/FAQ pages, PitchBook, TheRobotReport). Genuine competitor in the broad blind-mobility space, but overlap with Second Eye is LOWER than the dossier's medium rating once form factor and business model are examined.

FORM FACTOR — fundamentally different. Glide is a self-guided two-wheeled robot on a telescoping handle that physically rolls ahead and pulls the user along a route — a powered white-cane/guide-dog replacement. Second Eye is a head-worn wearable (glasses + neck box) giving bone-conduction obstacle alerts. Glide occupies the user's hand; a buyer could own both. Different physical category, not mutually exclusive purchases.

BUSINESS MODEL — opposite of ours, and this is our wedge. Glide requires a MANDATORY $30/month subscription to function (FAQ: subscription covers 'ongoing use of Glide and all of its functionality, connectivity, and updates') plus a smartphone app. We are free/no-subscription for the complete offline safety base — a direct marketing contrast. For a blind user, 'safety you pay monthly rent on' is an objection we don't carry.

OFFLINE — both are partly offline. Glide's core obstacle avoidance / walk-straight / line-of-sight targeting work offline; only the full AI suite needs signal. So it is NOT the pure cloud-tethered device the dossier ('offline: no') implied — correct that to partial. But its premium wayfinding and subscription gate keep it cloud-dependent for its headline value, whereas our full base is offline by design.

PRICE — same ballpark upfront but pricier in TCO: ~$1,199–$1,500 + $360/yr forever vs our sub-$700 one-time no-subscription target. We undercut on both axes.

STATUS — still PRE-LAUNCH, no units with customers as of mid-2026. Preorders launched Jul 2024, sold out Dec 2024, now CLOSED; Pathfinder cohort est. EARLY 2027 against a 'revised Spring 2026 production timeline'; first unit assembled Apr 2026. Repeated slips (CES 2024 → 2026/2027). We are not behind a shipping incumbent.

Net: credible, well-covered startup, same end-user, but different device class and the polar-opposite (subscription-required, cloud-gated) model. More a market-validation signal and marketing foil than a head-to-head wearable threat. Threat = LOW.
corrections: 1) PRICE outdated: dossier '$1,499 + $30/mo' → current primary source is $1,199 device-only (limited-time 20% off ~$1,499 MSRP), or bundles $1,500 (1yr) / $2,350 (4yr); +$30/mo still mandatory. 2) OFFLINE wrong: dossier 'offline: no' → FAQ confirms core obstacle avoidance/walk-straight/line-of-sight work OFFLINE with no connectivity; only full AI suite needs signal → should be PARTIAL. 3) STATUS/timeline outdated: dossier 'demo days through 2025, not yet shipping' → preorders launched Jul 2024, sold out Dec 2024, preorder now CLOSED, Pathfinder delivery est. EARLY 2027, revised Spring 2026 production timeline; first unit assembled Apr 2026. Still pre-launch. 4) FUNDING understated: not just the $1.5M pre-seed — ~$7.21M total raised per PitchBook (15+ investors incl. Outlander VC, Break Trail, Beyond Capital). 5) OVERLAP/THREAT: dossier rated both 'medium' → downgrade to LOW; different device class (rolling handheld robot vs wearable glasses) and opposite subscription-required/cloud-gated model make it a marketing foil more than a direct wearable competitor.

name: Microsoft Seeing AI / Google Lookout (free phone apps)
one_liner: Free phone apps owning reading/scene — Seeing AI has on-device document/scene modes, Lookout partial offline; they set the 'reading is free' floor that kills paid reading hardware.
form_factor: Smartphone app (phone-in-hand); some on-device modes
target_users: Blind and low-vision globally
key_features: Instant text, document, scene, currency, color, product/barcode, people recognition. Some functions run on-device (no network); not real-time hands-free walking. They commoditize reading/scene to zero — the structural reason our free reading-bonus must never be the pitch.
why:
Both real, free, actively shipping in 2026, both giants (Microsoft / Google). Confirmed from primary sources: Seeing AI is free, no subscription, on iOS + Android (Android GA via Microsoft Accessibility blog); Google Lookout free, no subscription, 7 modes (Text/Documents/Explore/Currency/Food labels/Find/Images), 30+ languages, Android 6+. "partial offline" is ACCURATE and the correct nuance: Lookout's core text/document reading runs on-device (Google support: core experience processed on device, usable without internet; Food labels works offline after a data download) but Explore/scene descriptions, conversational AI, and face recognition need internet. Same shape for Seeing AI per 2026 tested roundups (chance.vision, aithinkerlab): strongest offline TEXT reading among free tools, advanced scene/chat features cloud-bound.

WHY ONLY MEDIUM / LOW OVERLAP — the differentiation thesis holds: BOTH are point-and-scan phone-in-hand apps, NOT real-time hands-free walking navigation or obstacle avoidance. They own reading/scene/OCR; they do NOT do mobility/avoidance, which is our wedge. Direct product overlap is genuinely low. They are a MEDIUM threat not as a competitor but as a PRICE-FLOOR SETTER: two giants give reading/OCR/scene-description away free, forever, strategically. That permanently commoditizes the reading/OCR/scene-description category to $0. Strategic implication (matches the original note): our free offline reading/OCR/scene must be framed as a bonus, never the pitch — the moment we position reading as the value prop, we're competing against free Microsoft+Google and lose. Our defensible value must stay mobility-safety-grade real-time avoidance (hands-free, no phone) + future cloud agent. The original assessment is well-calibrated; no corrections needed.
corrections: None. Original claim is accurate on every checked dimension: both apps real and shipping in 2026, free, no subscription, partial/on-device offline (strong for text reading, cloud for advanced scene/face/chat), giants, point-and-scan (not hands-free mobility) form factor, low direct overlap, medium threat as $0 price-floor setters for reading/scene. Minor precision: Lookout offline is confirmed via Google support for core text + Food labels (offline after data download); Seeing AI's per-feature offline split is documented mainly via secondary 2026 tested roundups rather than a Microsoft per-feature page, but the "partial offline" characterization is correct.

name: Meta Ray-Ban / Oakley Meta (with Be My Eyes + Detailed Responses)
one_liner: Mainstream camera glasses that, via free Be My Eyes integration and a 2025 'Detailed Responses' accessibility mode, became the default cheap scene-describer for blind users — but does NOT do real-time mobility/obstacle detection.
form_factor: Single-piece camera glasses (~50g), open-ear speakers, phone-tethered
price_usd: $299 base Meta glasses / $379 Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2 / $499 Oakley Meta Vanguard / $799 Ray-Ban Display + Neural Band
target_users: Mainstream consumers; blind/low-vision as accessibility add-on (reading, scene, call-a-volunteer)
key_features: Be My Eyes call-a-volunteer by voice; Meta AI scene description; 'Detailed Responses' verbose mode (May/Jun 2025); free to blind end-user; huge distribution (Ray-Ban/Oakley/EssilorLuxottica retail)
why:
REAL and shipping at massive scale. Primary sources (Meta's own accessibility page, Be My Eyes, AFB AccessWorld Fall 2025, Consumer Reports) all confirm the product and its accessibility positioning. The card's central thesis is CORRECT and well-sourced: these are the default cheap scene-describer for blind users (Be My Eyes call-a-volunteer + Meta AI scene description + "Detailed Responses" verbose mode), free to the blind end-user, and they explicitly do NOT do real-time mobility/obstacle detection.

Why HIGH threat despite LOW feature overlap: the threat is distribution + the "good-enough, free, already-on-their-face" anchor, not capability overlap. Independent blind-user reviews converge:
- Consumer Reports (legally blind tester Lachi): "a helpful tool, a conversation starter" but "didn't replace a sighted companion"; still needed her cane + spatial awareness; requires Wi-Fi (not in airports/subways/rural); no proactive hazard warning, user must ask.
- AFB AccessWorld (Fall 2025): "Meta AI is not yet able to provide reliable navigation directions or replace tools like a white cane"; processing "fairly quick, but not instantaneous or accurate enough for something like street crossings."

This is exactly our wedge (real-time, sub-second, offline, safety-grade obstacle avoidance via on-device YOLO + ToF). Meta structurally can't match short-term: cloud-dependent (no offline), reactive query model (not a continuous proactive depth/obstacle stream), no depth sensor. So feature OVERLAP is genuinely LOW (downgraded the card's "medium" — they own scene-description/OCR/find-things, we own mobility-safety; the only real collision is our FUTURE platform layer of OCR/scene-description, where they already dominate on distribution).

Strategic implication: do NOT compete on scene-description (lost on distribution). Win on mobility-safety + offline + free-no-subscription, and frame Second Eye as complementary to a cane, not to Meta. Risk to watch: if Meta adds a ToF/depth sensor or a continuous proactive "watch mode," overlap jumps to high overnight — they have the distribution to make any feature default. This is the #1 competitor to track.
corrections: 1) overlap_with_us: card said "medium" — corrected to LOW. They do scene-description/OCR/call-a-volunteer; we do real-time mobility/obstacle. Near-zero feature collision today; collision is only in our future platform layer. 2) price: card's "$299-$379 (Display+Neural Band $799)" is right but incomplete — current lineup is $299 base Meta / $379 Ray-Ban Meta Gen 2 / $499 Oakley Meta Vanguard / $799 Ray-Ban Display. Meta shipped a new $299 line on 2026-06-23 (CNBC). 3) "Detailed Responses" date: card says May/Jun 2025 — plausible but I could NOT confirm the exact launch month from a primary source (Meta's page lists the feature as live; AFB/CR reviews don't date it). Treat the specific month as unverified; the feature's existence IS confirmed. 4) Be My Eyes integration: card frames it as "call-a-volunteer." Confirmed — and note Be My Eyes' AI does NOT run on the glasses, only live volunteer calls do (per Be My Eyes), which further limits their autonomous-assist overlap with us. A March 2026 update expanded it (group calling, direct brand service centers). 5) form_factor "single-piece ~50g, phone-tethered" — confirmed accurate; this is also a contrast point (our split-design offloads compute to a neck unit for on-device mobility AI they can't run on a 50g frame).

name: Google Android XR / Project Astra / Gemini glasses
one_liner: Google's Gemini-powered glasses platform (Warby Parker/Gentle Monster, audio + in-lens display), with Project Astra explicitly co-developed with Aira for blind/low-vision real-time scene Q&A.
form_factor: Audio-only and in-lens-display glasses; phone + cloud
price_usd: unannounced; analyst estimates $600–$900 for audio glasses (display variant likely higher). Aira AI sessions free during Trusted Tester phase.
target_users: Mainstream; accessibility/blind as a named use case via Astra + Aira partnership
key_features: Gemini multimodal vision, contextual memory, real-time object recognition, Maps/Lens; Trusted Tester program with Aira for blind users; OEM eyewear partners
why:
REAL and accurately described — confirmed against primary sources (Google blog, DeepMind, Aira). Android XR Gemini glasses were formally announced at Google I/O 2026 (May 2026); audio glasses ship "fall 2026," with OEMs Samsung/Qualcomm (platform) + Warby Parker/Gentle Monster (frames), iOS+Android pairing. Project Astra is genuinely co-developed with Aira as "Aira AI Visual Interpreter," explicitly for blind/low-vision, currently in an invite-only Trusted Tester waitlist (free during testing).

Why HIGH threat: (1) Distribution + brand — Google can put assistive AI glasses in every optician (Warby Parker) and onto every Android phone, a reach we cannot match. (2) It directly names blind/low-vision as a use case via Aira, our exact beachhead, so it competes for the same early-adopter mindshare and the same accessibility-funding/insurance channels. (3) Aira already has the VI relationships and reimbursement footprint we'd need to build.

Why NOT a full overlap (the wedge that keeps us alive): Architecturally it is the OPPOSITE of us. It is cloud-dependent (Gemini Nano on-device only for trivial routing; all real vision Q&A streams to cloud Gemini) — so it is NOT offline and is useless in a basement, subway, dead-zone, or on a dropped connection. It is built for mainstream "ask Gemini what you see" scene-Q&A and conversational interpretation, NOT real-time mobility-safety (no ToF depth, no sub-second bone-conduction "step ahead" obstacle warning — Aira itself disclaims it "cannot match trained interpreters" and is a convenience tool, not a safety device). Pricing is mainstream-premium ($600–900) with a subscription-minutes model (Aira) layered on, vs. our free, no-subscription, offline-complete safety baseline. Net: Google validates the market and raises the bar on cloud features, but leaves the offline + mobility-safety-grade + free-baseline niche open — that's precisely our defensible wedge. Treat as the category-defining incumbent to differentiate against, not a head-on mobility competitor.
corrections: Two corrections to the input record: (1) status/date — input said only "pre-launch"; more precisely it was OFFICIALLY ANNOUNCED at Google I/O 2026 (May 2026) with a named fall-2026 ship window for audio glasses — still pre-launch but no longer just rumored/previewed. (2) subscription — input said "unknown"; the glasses subscription model isn't announced, but the blind-user path (Aira AI / Project Astra) runs on Aira, which IS a paid minutes-based subscription product (free only during the current Trusted Tester phase). So mark subscription "optional/yes via Aira," not "unknown." price_usd "unknown" should be updated to "$600–900 analyst estimate, official TBD." offline="no" confirmed correct (cloud-dependent). overlap_with_us="medium" and threat_level="high" both confirmed correct.

Three structural reasons it does not contest our wedge: (a) NOT OFFLINE — Jinju is explicitly cloud/phone-dependent and useless without a paired Galaxy phone, the exact opposite of our offline, safety-grade mobility value prop for the blind; a blind user cannot trust a connectivity-dependent obstacle warning. corrections: 1) PRODUCT IS REAL but still a LEAK, not officially launched — all specs are rumored/unconfirmed by Samsung. Codename "Jinju" confirmed across Engadget, Tom's Guide, Android Central, Smartprix, etc. 2) PRICE: claim says "~$400"; consistent reporting is $379–$499 for the audio-only Jinju model (a more premium display model "Haean" is $600–$900). name: Ara by Strap Technologies
one_liner: Chest-worn obstacle-detection wearable (ultrasonic + lidar, haptic feedback) claiming ~80% fewer hits; $2,750, early-stage limited batches — pricier and not glasses/hands-free.
form_factor: Chest-worn unit with ultrasonic + lidar sensors; haptic feedback; standalone
price_usd: $2,750 (— $500 with code "Earlybird500"; $600 downpayment reserves a unit, balance due when device ships)
target_users: Blind/low-vision mobility; obstacle avoidance

why: SAME wedge (real-time mobility / obstacle avoidance for blind+low-vision via haptic feedback) and same core safety promise ("~80% fewer hits"), so it validates our market and is a direct functional competitor on mobility. But it is NOT a close substitute and the input's "overlap=high" overstates it — key divergences in OUR favor: (1) Form factor — they are a bulky neck+waist HARNESS; we are lightweight glasses + neck-box (~30g frame), far more socially acceptable and hands/torso-free. (2) Sensing — they are deliberately CAMERA-FREE (ultrasonic+LiDAR only), which caps them at "something is there" obstacle alerts; our camera + on-device YOLO + ToF enables a real platform (OCR, scene description, find-object, traffic-light, indoor nav) they architecturally cannot reach. (3) Price/business model — they are $2,750 one-time with a $600 downpayment, premium/niche; we are sub-$700 freemium with a FREE fully-offline safety tier — roughly 4x cheaper and a fundamentally different go-to-market (accessibility-as-safety should not be paywalled). (4) Maturity — both early, but they are thinly funded (~$1.3M disclosed, 2021) and shipping only in 250-unit batches over 4+ years, i.e. slow execution, not a scaling juggernaut. THREAT = MEDIUM, not high: they prove demand and are a credible incumbent reference customers will cite, and their "no-camera privacy" angle is a real counter-narrative we must answer (camera privacy in airports/homes). But on form factor, price, offline-free model, and especially platform extensibility we out-position them; they are camera-free by design so they can never become the multi-feature agent we're building. Watch items: any pivot to glasses, any camera addition, or a large new raise would raise the threat.
corrections: FORM FACTOR (input wrong): it is NOT a "chest-worn unit." Ara is a neck-and-waist HARNESS with sensors covering head/chest/foot levels; standalone, no phone needed. SENSORS: ultrasonic + LiDAR confirmed, and crucially it is explicitly CAMERA-FREE (marketed as a privacy advantage for airports/home). OFFLINE (input "unknown" -> confirmed YES): strap.tech states it "does not require constant internet connectivity"; core obstacle detection is on-device/standalone. SUBSCRIPTION (input "unknown" -> NO): one-time hardware purchase, no subscription found anywhere. PRICE/discount: $2,750 confirmed; current live code is "Earlybird500" = $500 off (input said codes ~$500 off — correct); $600 downpayment model confirmed. STATUS: shipping but in small batches (250-unit batches; Batch 0/1 sold out, Batch 2 open) — still early-stage hardware. FUNDING: only verifiable round is a $1.3M Seed (Apr 2021, led by Platform Capital); no large raise found — they partly self-fund via $600 customer downpayments. So "VC early-stage" is technically true but the company is thinly capitalized (~$1.3M disclosed, 5 yrs ago). Company is REAL (Austin TX + Guadalajara MX, 6 patents, Time Best Inventions 2022, SXSW Best of Show, Fast Company World Changing Ideas) — not a hallucination.
