Same price band as us (sub-$700) and same buyer (blind/low-vision), so it shares the storefront and channels. BUT the core overlap thesis is overstated and must be downgraded from "high" to "medium": (1) The "on-device Snapdragon, runs offline" claim is REFUTED by the primary source. AGIGA's own FAQ states "the glasses require a cloud connection for advanced AI features" and they are only "actively developing an offline OCR/Reading mode for a future update." Off-grid it degrades to a Bluetooth speaker + local photo/video. The Snapdragon/on-device line only appears in secondary aggregator/promo copy (NextBigFuture), never on AGIGA's site — treat as unverified marketing, not fact. (2) Zero obstacle detection, ToF depth, or mobility/navigation anywhere across 5+ sources — it is button-press scene-description + OCR reading, exactly the non-mobility segment. Our wedge (offline mobility-safety: YOLO+ToF -> bone-conduction obstacle alerts) does NOT overlap their product; we overlap only on the future-software roadmap (OCR/scene/Aira-style hooks), which is cloud-bound for them. Net: EchoVision is a credible, funded, already-shipping competitor for the READING/SCENE buyer and validates the category, but it is the inverse of our offline + mobility-safety positioning. Our two hardest differentiators — fully offline core and mobility-grade safety — are precisely where they are weakest (cloud-dependent, no obstacle sensing). They are the company to benchmark our scene/OCR roadmap against, not a head-to-head threat to the mobility wedge.
corrections: 1) "offline: partial" -> WRONG, should be NO. AGIGA store says "In stock" and is fulfilling Pioneer Edition to US/Canada; V1 Edition (9h battery vs 6h) next. 4) Subscription detail: now an explicit freemium with a named paid tier — free "Explorer" ($0) vs "Independence" $9.99/month — not merely "no sub for early adopters." 5) "overlap_with_us: high / threat_level: high" -> downgrade both to MEDIUM: no mobility/ToF/obstacle features, and they are cloud-bound where we are offline. 6) Funding: keep modest — Valkyrie Fund (early-stage SV); PitchBook hints ~$4.2M, Tracxn shows no formal round. one_liner: Xiaomi's planned dual-chip camera AI glasses (AR nav, translation, object ID, electrochromic lenses, 'affordable') — a giant + supply-chain juggernaut entering cheap AI glasses; mainstream, not blind/mobility, but compresses the low-price tier.
form_factor: Camera glasses, dual-chip, electrochromic tint; phone + cloud
target_users: Mainstream consumers
key_features: AR navigation, real-time translation, object recognition, one-tap lens tint. General-purpose; Xiaomi's pricing/scale is a structural threat to anyone competing on cheap glasses hardware

name: Shanghai Jiao Tong University AI obstacle-glasses (academic)
one_liner: SJTU research mounting tiny on-device AI on discontinued Google Glass prototypes for obstacle detection with bone-conduction alerts — nearly identical technical recipe to ours, signaling our approach is sound but not unique; lab-stage, not a product.
form_factor: Google Glass prototype + tiny onboard AI computer + bone conduction
target_users: Research subjects (blind/low-vision)
key_features: On-glasses AI surveys environment; bone-conduction prompt on approaching obstacle — same camera+on-device+bone-conduction recipe as Second Eye. Validates feasibility but shows the idea is in the academic air; no commercialization/price
why: Low threat. This is academic research (Nature Machine Intelligence, Apr 2025), not a company — no product, no price, no entity, no go-to-market, no funding round. It cannot take customers from us. Its strategic value to us is VALIDATION, not competition: an independent, peer-reviewed, top-journal team converged on the same core recipe (depth camera + on-device inference + non-visual feedback for blind mobility), which de-risks our technical bet. But the original record overstated overlap as "high" — it's actually MEDIUM: (a) their differentiator is wrist-worn electronic-skin HAPTICS for turn-by-turn steering, a different modality than our bone-conduction-first audio approach; (b) ~200g full-system weight vs our lightweight split-frame (~30g glasses + neck box) — they are not optimizing for consumer wearable comfort; (c) it's a single research artifact, not a platform/freemium/medical-expansion business like ours; (d) the "Google Glass" overlap detail was a secondary-source error, so the "nearly identical hardware" framing is overstated. Net: useful credibility tailwind (cite when investors ask "is this feasible?") and a talent-watch signal (Gu Leilei's lab), not a market threat. Re-rate if the team spins out a company.
corrections:
1) REAL, not a hallucination — but the headline "Google Glass prototype" detail is WRONG/unsupported. The peer-reviewed primary source (Nature Machine Intelligence vol 7 no 4 pp 627-638, "Human-centred design and fabrication of a wearable multimodal visual assistance system," published 14 Apr 2025; DOI 10.1038/s42256-025-01018-6) describes a CUSTOM-BUILT wearable, not a repurposed/discontinued Google Glass. The "Google Glass" framing appears only in one secondary write-up, not the paper. Drop it. 2) Lead researcher: Gu Leilei (顾磊磊), associate professor at SJTU, collaborating with Fudan, HKUST, and East China Normal University — not a solo SJTU effort. 3) Form factor: smart glasses with an RGB-D (depth) camera mounted between the eyebrows + a credit-card-sized single-board computer + TWO wrist-worn artificial electronic-skin haptic patches (left/right/forward steering) + bone-conduction audio; full system ~200g. It is a multimodal haptic+audio system, not a pure bone-conduction-prompt device. 4) status "unknown" -> pre-launch / research-stage: ~18 blind/low-vision participants tested in virtual + real-world settings; no commercialization, no spin-out, no price, no disclosed funding beyond university research. 5) offline "yes" -> PARTIAL/unconfirmed: on-device single-board-computer inference is consistent with edge/offline operation, but the paper does not explicitly assert a fully-offline guarantee like our product does. 6) overlap_with_us "high" -> MEDIUM (different feedback modality, far heavier, research-only, Google-Glass detail was an error).</corrections>

name: Vuzix (healthcare smart glasses: M400 / Shield / LX1)
one_liner: Public US smart-glasses maker; the dominant hardware platform under most hospital telemedicine/remote-assist and vitals-on-glasses deployments.
price_usd: ~$1,800 (M400 Xtreme kit, vuzix.com); enterprise units span ~$1,500–2,500+. LX1 price not public.
form_factor: Monocular assisted-reality glasses + waveguide display
key_features: Hands-free audio/video, remote expert link, Teams/Zoom native, AI app platform (TeleVU etc.); used for surgical instruction across Asian hospitals; LX1 got FCC/CE Feb 2026.
target_users: Hospitals, surgeons, field clinicians, telemedicine
why: REAL and verified against primary sources — not a hallucination. Public company (NASDAQ:VUZI), healthcare line live on vuzix.com (M400 flagship, HIPAA/IP67, surgery+telemedicine+training, "patient info in field of vision," vitals monitoring), Asian-hospital surgical-instruction PR genuine (Jul 2024, M400, 4K live surgical broadcast), LX1 FCC/CE genuine (Feb 4 2026, shipping). BUT overlap with Second Eye is LOW, not medium — they sit in a fundamentally different market on every axis that matters: (1) BUYER & USER — Vuzix sells to hospitals/enterprises so a SIGHTED clinician (surgeon, field tech) gets a heads-up DISPLAY; our buyer is a BLIND/low-vision individual who gets AUDIO (bone-conduction) mobility guidance. A waveguide monocular display is useless to a blind user — opposite modality. (2) JOB-TO-BE-DONE — theirs is "stream my POV to a remote expert / overlay data for a sighted wearer"; ours is "keep a blind person from hitting obstacles, fully offline." (3) ARCHITECTURE — Vuzix's core value (Teams/Zoom remote-assist link, telemedicine) is connectivity-dependent BY DESIGN; our wedge is offline mobility-safety. (4) PRICE/MODEL — ~$1,800+ enterprise hardware vs our sub-$700 freemium consumer device. The ONLY real adjacency is our planned hospital-caretaker / "vitals-on-glasses" expansion — Vuzix is the incumbent display platform there and would be a credible competitor IF we pivot up-market into clinical AR. On our actual wedge (blind mobility) there is no competition from them. Financially they are weak (rev ~$6.3M, negative gross margin, ~-$6.7M Q1'26 FCF, ~$2.79 stock) — an entrenched but struggling niche hardware vendor, not a fast-moving threat to our beachhead.
corrections: 1) Price floor: M400 kit is ~$1,799 (per vuzix.com product page), so the listed "~$1,500-2,500+" lower bound is right for older config but the shipping kit anchors near $1,800. 2) LX1 cert date is precisely Feb 4, 2026 (claim's "Feb 2026" is correct, just imprecise). 3) "Shield" model in the name was NOT corroborated in current sources — Vuzix's healthcare/enterprise lineup today is M400, M4000, LX1, Blade 2; "Shield" appears to be a stale/incorrect model reference (possibly confused with a defense product). 4) overlap_with_us downgraded from "medium" to "low" — different modality (display vs audio), user (sighted clinician vs blind person), and architecture (cloud remote-assist vs offline). 5) threat_level downgraded medium→low for the same reason plus their weak financials; only becomes medium IF we pivot into the clinical/caretaker AR segment.

name: TeleVU Innovation (uSee / iSee on Vuzix)
one_liner: Software layer on Vuzix glasses that explicitly lets a clinician monitor patient vital signs hands-free without taking eyes off the patient — the closest match to our caregiver-vitals-on-glasses concept.
category: Medical AR glasses software (remote care + vitals overlay)
price_usd: undisclosed (enterprise/quote; M400 ~$1,800-2,000 hardware + per-seat SaaS; $3,000 PoC credit offered)
form_factor: Vuzix glasses + cloud app
key_features: Live audio/video to remote expert + AR; 'monitor vital signs without taking eyes or hands off the patient'; long-term home care, hospital care, first responders, mobile crisis.
target_users: Home care, hospitals, first responders, remote clinics
why: 真实存在、在售，但重叠被高估。详见结构化字段。TeleVU 是加拿大 bootstrapped 医疗 telepresence 公司（iSee/uSee on Vuzix M400 + 独立 RPM 设备 BioVU Quattro），云依赖+订阅+B2B，无 mobility/避障/离线/盲人辅助/freemium。原卡片"closest match to caregiver-vitals-on-glasses"高估——其 vitals 措辞实为 Vuzix 通用营销语，眼镜端无实时体征 HUD；体征靠独立 RPM 硬件。对我们 mobility wedge 零威胁，仅远期医疗扩张时为远房竞品。Overlap high→low，threat medium→low。
corrections:
1) 原卡片 one_liner 称其"explicitly lets a clinician monitor patient vital signs hands-free... closest match to our caregiver-vitals-on-glasses concept" —— 高估。该 vitals 措辞是 Vuzix 通用营销语，TeleVU 眼镜端并无实时体征 HUD 叠加；其体征由独立 RPM 设备 BioVU Quattro 提供。
2) 原卡片 sources 两条 URL 现均 404（vuzix.com blog 与 ir.vuzix.com 详情页都失效）。合作事实改由 PRNewswire 官方稿（2021-07-20）佐证；vitals 措辞改由 vuzix.com/pages/healthcare 佐证。
3) funding "unknown" → 更准确为"no disclosed funding raised"（Crunchbase/Tracxn，bootstrapped）。
4) overlap_with_us 应从 high 降为 low；threat_level 从 medium 降为 low。
5) Vuzix 是合作硬件方而非 TeleVU 自研，TeleVU 是软件+RPM 设备方。

name: Masimo (W1 Medical Watch + SafetyNet, Radius VSM)
one_liner: Patient-monitoring giant moving into wearables; W1 wrist watch (SpO2/PR/Pi/HR) FDA-cleared to feed its SafetyNet telemonitoring cloud reviewed by remote caregivers.
price_usd: ~$499 (W1 consumer hardware); SafetyNet membership $7.99/mo basic or $18.99/mo advanced ($79/$189 annual); Radius VSM + Patient SafetyNet is enterprise/hospital priced (quote on request)
form_factor: Medical wrist watch (MW-1 module) + Radius VSM body sensor + SafetyNet cloud
key_features: 30+ yrs SET pulse-ox + rainbow CO-oximetry, high-res SpO2/PR/Pi/HR; clinician web portal anywhere. A monitoring incumbent — owns the vitals-data + clinician-dashboard value chain a glasses overlay would have to plug into or displace.
target_users: Hospitals, clinics, long-term care, home
why:
REAL and shipping — not a hallucination. Verified against Masimo primary sources: W1 Medical Watch (FDA-cleared, ~$499, SpO2/PR/Pi/HR), Radius VSM body sensor (active 2024-25 hospital deployments — Vanderbilt ER protocol Oct 2024, University Hospitals 1,500 beds Oct 2025), and SafetyNet cloud telemonitoring/telehealth. Masimo is a multi-billion-$ NASDAQ:MASI incumbent (30+ yrs SET pulse-ox).

But the real overlap with Second Eye is LOW and the threat is LOW, not medium. Different organ entirely: Masimo measures the wearer's own vitals via wrist/chest sensors and pipes them to a clinician dashboard over Wi-Fi/cellular+cloud. We do real-time outward-facing obstacle avoidance for blind users via head-worn camera + on-device YOLO + ToF, fully offline. Zero feature, form-factor, user, or distribution overlap today: no head-worn device, no vision/scene perception, no blind/low-vision product, and a cloud-mandatory architecture that is the inverse of our offline-free safety positioning.

The ONLY adjacency is speculative and lives entirely in OUR future roadmap (the 'blind → hospital caregivers viewing patient vitals on glasses' expansion). IF we pursue medical/caregiver glasses, Masimo owns the vitals-data + clinician-dashboard value chain we'd have to integrate with — but that's years out and a partner/integration target as much as a competitor. Critically, Masimo just SOLD its consumer business (Sound United → HARMAN, completed Sept 2025) to refocus on professional hospital monitoring — it is moving away from consumer wearables and toward the clinical-vitals stack, with no signal of entering vision-based assistive or head-worn devices. Watch as a long-horizon ecosystem/integration consideration for our caregiver pivot; not a competitive threat to the mobility wedge.
corrections: 1) Threat level lowered from medium to LOW. The card's own overlap rating (low) and the actual product domain (wrist/chest vitals telemetry for clinicians) don't support a medium threat to a blind-mobility obstacle-avoidance glasses product. There is no head-worn device, no vision/scene-understanding, no blind/low-vision use case anywhere in Masimo's line. 2) Pricing corrected/expanded: $499 is consumer hardware-only; the recurring cost is the SafetyNet membership ($7.99/$18.99 per month), not 'enterprise pricing' generically — confirmed verbatim from Masimo shop pages. 3) Status context: in Sept 2025 Masimo COMPLETED divestment of its consumer audio business (Sound United / Bowers & Wilkins, Denon, Marantz) to HARMAN/Samsung for ~$328M, explicitly to refocus on core professional healthcare. So Masimo is doubling down on the hospital vitals-monitoring/telehealth value chain — moving further AWAY from any consumer/wearable-glasses adjacency, not toward it. 4) 'subscription: optional' is accurate as written — base watch works without SafetyNet, but the clinician/telemonitoring value (the part this card frames as the moat) REQUIRES the paid cloud, which is the opposite of our offline-free positioning.

name: Rabbit R1
one_liner: $199 orange handheld 'Large Action Model' agent that learns to operate apps for you — the canonical learning-agent device, widely panned as half-baut but still shipping and iterating.
form_factor: Pocket handheld device with rotating camera, scroll wheel, small touchscreen (not a wearable)

key_features: LAM/DLAM 'large action model' that observes and operates web UIs and 'gets better over time' (the learning-agent angle), cloud AI, Rabbit Intern, camera

But it's cloud-only, non-wearable, sighted-consumer, and reputationally damaged (LAM-or-scam reviews). Lesson: a self-improving-agent claim must actually work or it becomes a liability; for the blind, reliability bar is far higher.

name: Bee (Amazon)
one_liner: $50 always-listening memory bracelet that learns your routines — cheap, beloved entry point now owned by Amazon (acquired July 2025), making the giant a direct player in personalized AI wearables.
form_factor: Wristband or clip pendant; also Apple Watch app
funding: Acquired by Amazon (July 2025); marks Amazon's return to wearables after Halo
target_users: Sighted consumers wanting a passive 'second memory' / personal agentic AI
key_features: Always-on listening, transcribes conversations, learns routines/preferences, summaries/reminders/to-dos, 'truly personal agentic AI' (Amazon stated direction)
threat_reasoning: Functional overlap low (audio memory, no camera, no mobility, sighted users). Threat is MEDIUM only via framework ②: Amazon now owns a cheap, mass-distribution personal-agent wearable and explicitly wants 'agentic AI for more customers' — if Amazon ever bolts vision/accessibility onto it (Alexa+ accessibility), the learning-agent layer we plan could be commoditized. Watch, don't fight on their turf.
why:
Real and current. Bee Pioneer ($49.99 wristband/clip) confirmed shipping as of June 2026; acquired by Amazon July 2025; still operational under Amazon, showcased at CES Jan 2026 with an 8-person SF team (TechCrunch 2026-01-12, Forbes 2026-01-12). Requires a $19/mo subscription for full/premium features. NOT offline: it does real-time cloud transcription of audio and requires connectivity — opposite of our offline-safety positioning. Direct functional overlap with us is LOW: audio-only memory wearable, no camera today, no depth/ToF, no mobility/obstacle function, aimed at sighted consumers wanting a passive 'second memory,' not blind/low-vision users. Threat stays MEDIUM via the giant-distribution + learning-agent vector, and is now MORE concrete than the original card assumed: (1) Amazon owns a cheap, mass-distribution personal-agent wearable and explicitly wants 'agentic AI for more customers,' so the self-learning/personalization agent layer we plan to differentiate on could be commoditized at $50; (2) IMPORTANT correction — Bee's original prototype DID include vision/camera, shelved only for startup cost reasons, and co-founder Maria de Lourdes Zollo says future Bee-family devices may add cameras, with Amazon (Alexa+ accessibility, deep pockets) now backing it. So the 'if Amazon bolts vision on' scenario is a stated direction, not hypothetical. Still: even camera-equipped, it would target sighted convenience/lifestyle, is cloud-dependent (no mobility-safety-grade offline guarantee), and has no ToF/real-time obstacle pipeline. Posture: watch closely (esp. any accessibility/vision announcement), don't fight on their audio-memory turf; our moat = offline mobility-safety + no-subscription base + depth sensing.</why_threat_or_not>
<parameter name="subscription">yes
corrections:
1) Original card framed Amazon adding vision as purely hypothetical ('IF Amazon ever bolts vision on'); in fact Bee's prototype already had a camera (shelved for cost) and the co-founder publicly says future Bee-family devices may include cameras — this is a stated/likely direction, so the medium threat is better-grounded than the card implies. 2) Subscription is required for full/premium functionality, not merely an add-on — confirmed still $19/mo post-acquisition. 3) Acquisition and shipping status both re-confirmed current as of Jan 2026 (CES), not stale. Everything else in the card (price $49.99, offline=no, status=shipping, giant-owned, overlap=low) checks out.</corrections>

name: Limitless (Meta)
one_liner: Personalized memory pendant ('powered by what you've seen, said, and heard') — the most polished memory-agent wearable, acquired by Meta in Dec 2025, putting a second giant squarely in personal-AI hardware.
form_factor: Clip-on aluminum pendant + companion app
price_usd: Hardware no longer sold (was $99–$199, briefly listed $399). Subscriptions waived: existing users moved to Unlimited free for ≥1yr (was Free 20hr/mo, Pro ~$20/mo, Unlimited ~$29/mo)
funding: Acquired by Meta (Dec 2025); had raised ~$33M+ as Rewind/Limitless
target_users: Sighted knowledge-workers wanting an always-on personal memory/AI
key_features: Continuous capture, searchable personal memory, meeting summaries/action items, 'personalized AI powered by your life' — a learning/personalization agent; freemium tier model
threat_reasoning: Borrowable, not threatening on function (no camera/mobility/accessibility). Two takeaways: (1) its freemium structure (free basic AI hours + paid unlimited) is a close model for our freemium cloud tier — worth studying; (2) now Meta-owned, so the 'personalized learning agent' vector is being absorbed by a giant — reinforces ② (don't pitch ourselves as a generic memory agent).
why: LOW threat, both functionally and now structurally. Functionally near-zero overlap with us: Limitless is an audio-only memory/transcription pendant for sighted knowledge-workers — no camera, no depth/ToF, no obstacle avoidance, no mobility/accessibility. It cannot do the one thing our wedge requires (real-time visual hazard detection for the blind). Structurally it has now been absorbed by Meta (Dec 5, 2025), hardware sales halted, Rewind sunset, EU/UK pulled — so it is no longer even an independent competitor to track. Two borrowable takeaways survive and are the only reason to keep it on the radar: (a) Its PRE-acquisition freemium structure (free tier with capped hours + paid unlimited) is a clean reference model for our optional cloud tier — study it, but note it's now defunct as a live example. (b) The acquisition is a STRATEGIC SIGNAL, not a product threat: it confirms Meta is buying into personal-AI wearables and the "personalized learning agent" vector. That reinforces our positioning discipline — do NOT pitch Second Eye as a generic memory/personalization agent (giants own that lane); stay on the mobility-safety, offline, accessibility wedge they cannot/ will not serve. As a buyable product it is effectively DEAD/absorbed into Meta, not shipping. Existing users get ~1yr support only. 2) SUBSCRIPTION: claim says "optional" — now effectively "no": subscriptions waived, existing users get Unlimited free. (The freemium tiers the claim cites — free 20hr/mo + Pro/Unlimited — were real PRE-acquisition.) 3) PRICE: claim's "$99–$399" range is real historically but no longer purchasable. 4) Rewind app (non-Pendant desktop-recording software) is being wound down; EU/UK service withdrawn with data deletion after Dec 19, 2025. 5) "powered by what you've seen" framing overstates — it's AUDIO-ONLY, NO CAMERA (confirmed, IP54, identifies speakers from voice). why:
CONFIRMED real, shipping, and accurately described — highest threat in the set. It is the giant owning the camera-AI-glasses form factor at scale, at price points overlapping ours, and it ALREADY ships an accessibility on-ramp for the blind (Be My Eyes hands-free via "Hey Meta, Be My Eyes" + Meta AI "Detailed Responses" scene description / text reading) on the cheaper $299+ camera Ray-Bans. That erodes any reading/scene-description-based pitch to the blind for ~$300.

CRITICAL GAP (our entire defensibility): Meta's own blind/low-vision page lists NO real-time obstacle detection, NO ToF depth, NO bone-conduction mobility/safety alerts. Confirmed absent. Meta is a scene-understanding + remote-volunteer Q&A tool, not a mobility-safety device. A blind user still cannot trust it to say "step 1.5m ahead." Our wedge (MOBILITY/avoidance via YOLO+ToF→bone conduction) sits squarely in that gap.

OFFLINE = the sharpest differentiator. Meta is cloud-dependent: per Meta's own Help docs, AI features require Wi-Fi/data; only photo/video capture and 5-language (ES/FR/IT/DE/PT) translation work offline. For a blind user relying on it for safety, cloud dependency is disqualifying — our fully-offline base is a genuine, defensible distinction (verified, not asserted).

WHY STILL HIGH, NOT MEDIUM: Meta defines consumer expectations, has the EssilorLuxottica retail channel, and could add mobility/depth at any time (it has the cash and the camera). The threat is strategic/latent, not current-feature overlap. Mitigants: offline + mobility-safety-grade + no-subscription safety guarantee + future payer/medical channel (hospitals, caregivers) all sit outside Meta's mainstream-consumer model. Overlap stays MEDIUM (same form factor + same accessibility customer, different job-to-be-done).

Per Meta Help: photo/video capture works offline, and translation among ES/FR/IT/DE/PT works offline; everything else (Meta AI assistant, navigation, real-time AI, Be My Eyes, the 14 newer languages) requires cloud. The original "no" overstates Meta's cloud lock-in slightly — but the safety-relevant point (AI/scene/assistant features are cloud-dependent) is fully confirmed.
3) Be My Eyes integration confirmed and now broader than stated: as of March 2026 it also connects to trusted contacts and to enterprise support teams (Tesco, Sony, Amtrak, Hilton, etc.), available on Ray-Ban Meta AND Oakley Meta in 21 markets. Strengthens, not weakens, the threat reasoning.

why: Device is REAL (Bloomberg Law, MacRumors, 9to5Mac, court filings all confirm), but it is NOT a competitor to our blind+mobility wedge today and won't be soon. It targets ~800M mainstream sighted ChatGPT users as a general ambient companion; it is fundamentally cloud-dependent (OpenAI's models), has NO offline mode, NO obstacle-avoidance/ToF depth, NO accessibility/mobility-safety positioning. So functional overlap with our jobs (real-time avoidance for the blind, fully offline, safety-grade) stays LOW. Threat stays HIGH for a strategic, not functional, reason: when the biggest agentic-AI player ships an always-aware wearable companion at 40-50M scale, it DEFINES consumer expectations for 'a personal AI you wear' and for the always-learning agent layer — directly pressuring the value of our future 'cloud self-learning agent' as a differentiator, and making investors ask 'why won't OpenAI just do this?'. Strategic counter (unchanged): never position Second Eye as a general ambient assistant. Anchor hard on blind + mobility-safety + offline — the exact jobs a cloud-tethered, camera-light general companion explicitly will NOT do. One thing the original analysis got materially wrong and should be corrected internally: this device DOES have a camera + environmental sensors (contextual awareness is its headline), so don't lean on 'no camera' as the reason for low overlap — lean on cloud-dependence, no mobility/depth function, and mainstream-not-accessibility targeting.
corrections: Multiple factual errors in the supplied claim: (1) STATUS/TIMELINE STALE: claim says 'H2 2026'; per a Feb 2026 court filing the device will ship 'no earlier than the end of February 2027' (originally 2026, now slipped). Still pre-launch, but the H2-2026 date is outdated. (2) NAME IS WRONG: it will NOT be called 'io'. A federal judge — upheld by the 9th Circuit — BARRED OpenAI from using 'io'/'IYO' (any capitalization) after a trademark suit from hearing-aid/earbud startup iyO. Internal codename is 'Sweetpea'; public name is TBD. (3) FUNDING FIGURE OFF: claim says '$6.4B acquisition of io'; primary sources (Adafruit reporting on court docs) cite ~$6.5B for io Products/LoveFrom. (4) CAMERA CLAIM WRONG: claim asserts 'no camera/vision/mobility focus disclosed' and uses that for low overlap. Sources (Built In, byteiota, storyboard18, Axios-adjacent) confirm built-in CAMERAS + microphones + environmental sensors for contextual awareness — vision IS part of it. (5) FORM FACTOR CONTESTED: claim says 'pocket/neck-worn iPod-shuffle + pen Gumdrop'; the leading 2026 rumor is now EARBUDS ('Sweetpea') with mastoid EMG silent-speech, alongside pocket and pen variants — form factor is genuinely unconfirmed. CONFIRMED parts of the claim: real OpenAI+Jony Ive project; screenless; 40-50M unit first-year target via Foxconn; pre-launch; USA/OpenAI; mainstream sighted consumers; low functional overlap; high strategic threat.

name: SJTU Gu Leilei team — wearable multimodal visual assistance (Nature Machine Intelligence 2025)
one_liner: Academic prototype matching our exact stack — RGB-D glasses + bone-conduction voice + wrist e-skin vibration + on-device compute — published in Nature MI, April 2025; our biggest 'someone proved the architecture' signal.
form_factor: Glasses with RGB-D camera + 2 stretchable electronic-skin wrist actuators + Raspberry Pi micro-PC (~200g)
region: China (Shanghai Jiao Tong University / Shanghai AI Lab)
target_users: Blind / partially sighted, indoor + outdoor mobility
key_features: AI scene interpretation; 200-300ms photo-to-output latency (human-reaction-time match); bone-conduction voice + haptic e-skin; tested with 20 users (12 indoor maze, 8 real city streets). Still prototype — stability/safety lab-to-field gap is the stated blocker (same risk as us)
why:
CONFIRMED REAL via primary source. Peer-reviewed paper "Human-centred design and fabrication of a wearable multimodal visual assistance system," Nature Machine Intelligence, April 2025, corresponding author Leilei Gu (SJTU Qingyuan Research Institute / Shanghai AI Lab; collaborators Fudan, HKUST, ECNU). Architecture matches our stack almost exactly: 3D-printed glasses frame + RGB-D camera + on-device compute + bone-conduction audio + stretchable artificial-skin (e-skin) wrist haptics, with self-powered triboelectric smart insoles and a VR training platform. Physics World's coverage of the paper explicitly confirms "all computations performed locally without the need for a remote server" — so the OFFLINE claim is verified, the single biggest validation that our exact architecture (offline, multimodal mobility-safety) is technically sound and human-tested.

WHY ONLY LOW THREAT (not medium): This is an ACADEMIC PROTOTYPE, not a competitor in the commercial sense. As of June 2026 (14 months post-publication) there is NO company, NO spinoff, NO product, NO price, NO mass-production plan, and NO market timeline in any source I checked (EN + 中文, incl. targeted searches for 量产/商业化/公司). The authors themselves frame it as "a promising research prototype" and the lab-to-field stability/safety gap is the stated open problem. Chinese smart-glasses market reports from 2026 do not list this team as a shipping player. So it does not compete for our customers, channel, or capital — academics publish and move on; they rarely productize hardware-heavy assistive devices, and university IP licensing is slow. It is a SIGNAL, not a rival: it de-risks our thesis (someone proved offline RGB-D + bone-conduction + haptics works with real blind users) AND, because it is open published science, it is a resource we can cite/learn from rather than a force that takes our market. The residual risk is reputational/IP — they hold a strong prior-art / patent position on the e-skin actuator approach, and a well-funded Chinese OEM could license it; worth monitoring, but not a go-to-market threat to a sub-$700 freemium product today.
corrections:
Several corrections to the input record: (1) FEEDBACK CHANNELS — the e-skin is wrist-mounted vibration actuators (haptic) as stated; correct. But the cited "200-300ms photo-to-output latency" is NOT in the primary source I could verify — the only hard latency figure in the paper/Physics World coverage is 18 ms for the lateral-obstacle haptic response. The 200-300ms "human-reaction-time match" appears to be secondary-press paraphrase, not a paper-stated end-to-end figure; treat as UNVERIFIED. (2) COMPUTE HARDWARE — input says "Raspberry Pi micro-PC (~200g)." The paper describes the camera connected to a MICROCONTROLLER via USB for local compute; no source I found names a Raspberry Pi specifically. The ~200g is the TOTAL SYSTEM weight (glasses + compute + battery), reported in press, not the compute board alone. Lower confidence on "Raspberry Pi" — flag as unconfirmed. (3) USER TEST SPLIT — CONFIRMED accurate: 20 participants total, 12 in indoor maze, 8 on real city streets / furniture-cluttered rooms. (4) STATUS — "pre-launch" is technically the schema's best fit but slightly misleading; more precisely this is "academic, no commercialization intent announced," i.e. it is NOT on a launch track. (5) THREAT_LEVEL downgraded from input's "medium" to "low" — see why_threat_or_not.

REAL OVERLAP WITH US: The architecture overlap is genuinely high (this is the closest published match to our stack). But the COMPETITIVE overlap is low because they have no product and no path-to-market signal. Net: it is our strongest "the architecture works" proof point and a citable scientific asset, not a market rival.

name: Meta Ray-Ban / Oakley Meta + Be My Eyes
one_liner: THE giant in the room — mainstream camera glasses with 'Hey Meta, Be My Eyes' free volunteer/AI visual assistance; commoditizes reading/scene to free at scale, but explicitly canNOT do real-time mobility/obstacle/street-crossing — the gap we own.

why:
REAL and dominant — every material claim checks out against primary sources. The "Hey Meta, Call a Volunteer on Be My Eyes" hands-free integration launched Nov 13, 2024 (US/CA/UK/IE/AU), expanded March 2026 to 21 markets + AI scene/text reading + direct-to-brand support calls (bemyeyes.com, meta.com/ai-glasses/accessibility). All accessibility features are FREE to the glasses user (no subscription). This is the giant that commoditizes reading/scene-description/identify at consumer scale and price — the threat is real and structural, not a niche player.

WHY IT'S STILL "HIGH" YET ONLY "MEDIUM OVERLAP" — the gap we own is confirmed by a primary source, not just asserted: Consumer Reports (Lachi field test) confirms the glasses are "a descriptive AI tool rather than a mobility aid" — she "still needed my Glam Cane... still had to rely on my own spatial awareness." No real-time obstacle detection, no step/curb warnings, no street-crossing guarantee. That is exactly Second Eye's mobility-safety wedge.

TWO CONFIRMED MOATS THE GIANT CANNOT MATCH SOON:
1) OFFLINE — REFUTED for Meta. Be My Eyes on Meta glasses REQUIRES Bluetooth-to-phone + active Wi-Fi/cellular (it's cloud volunteer/AI calls); Meta AI itself needs internet (support.bemyeyes.com, meta.com). Our fully-offline avoidance loop has no dependency on connectivity — decisive for safety-critical mobility where a dead zone = a missed curb.
2) NO MOBILITY-SAFETY GRADE — Meta explicitly does NOT position this as a navigation/obstacle device; it's hands-free description. We do the job they refuse.

CAVEATS for our positioning, not contradictions:
- "Free + no subscription" is NOT our differentiator vs Meta — Meta's accessibility layer is also free/no-sub. Our differentiators are OFFLINE + MOBILITY-SAFETY + sub-$700 dedicated device. Drop "free basic no-subscription" from the head-to-head pitch; it doesn't separate us from the giant.

BOTTOM LINE: Don't fight head-on. The giant has made reading/scene-ID a free commodity — so do NOT pitch those as our core value. Own what they structurally won't: offline, real-time depth-based obstacle/step/curb avoidance with bone-conduction, safety-grade, no connectivity dependency. Watch for Meta adding any depth-sensor/ToF + on-device obstacle model — that would be the encroachment signal.

name: 科大讯飞 iFlytek — AI助残 (smart glasses + guide tools)
one_liner: Chinese AI giant with a state-backed disability-tech lab (with China Disabled Persons' Federation) building blind/low-vision smart glasses & canes — the giant-on-home-turf risk in China; currently translation-glasses-first, blind tools still early.

funding: iFlytek (public, multi-billion); state-backed via CDPF joint AGI-accessibility lab + subsidiary Aibo Intelligent
target_users: Motor/hearing/visual-impaired; mass China market
key_features: Spark (星火) LLM cross-modal image→text + controllable speech synthesis for blind/low-vision 'smart glasses & smart canes'; 2025 AI-accessibility innovation contest (incl. guide-dog robots); deep gov/CDPF distribution. The giant that can flood the China channel — direct competition there is to be avoided
why:
MEDIUM threat (downgraded from the source's "high"), and it's a future/strategic threat, not a present-day product collision.

Why still a real threat: (1) It is the giant-on-home-turf in China — public company, multi-billion, with the Spark LLM, 808 AI capabilities on its open platform, and millions of developers. (2) It is STATE-BACKED for exactly our user: a formal joint lab with the China Disabled Persons' Federation (CDPF) plus a dedicated subsidiary (讯飞爱博智能). CDPF controls China's disability distribution channel (procurement, subsidies, rehab centers) — if/when iFlytek ships a blind product, it can flood the China channel in a way a startup cannot match. (3) They have explicitly named blind/low-vision smart glasses AND smart canes as a target. This is the structural reason to avoid head-to-head competition in the China market.

Why NOT high right now: (1) Their blind glasses/canes do not exist as a product — pure "exploring" R&D, no unit, no price, no date. (2) Their actual shipping glasses are a translation/productivity device for SIGHTED users — a different category, buyer, and job-to-be-done; zero mobility/obstacle-avoidance features. (3) Architecturally they are cloud-Spark-LLM-first, which is the OPPOSITE of our offline + free-base-no-subscription + mobility-safety-grade wedge — a giant's cloud-AI instinct is poorly matched to a "must work in a tunnel with no signal, safety-critical, no paywall on safety" product. (4) The blind-assistance category in China is already contested by shipping players (智能助盲帽/中兴, WeWALK, BrainPort, Seeing AI), so iFlytek is not uniquely ahead.

Strategic implication for us: treat iFlytek as the reason NOT to make China the beachhead, and as a clock — they have the channel and the mandate, so our moat (offline, free safety baseline, mobility-grade latency, self-learning agent) must be built and defended in a market where they are weak (overseas / channels CDPF doesn't control) before they productize. They are a watch-closely, plan-around competitor — not a today-collision.
corrections:
CORRECTION 1 (status): The blind/low-vision product is NOT pre-launch-with-imminent-ship; it is early R&D. iFlytek's own 2025 newsroom describes it as "持续探索...为盲人或低视力用户打造...智能眼镜、智能盲杖等新工具" — "continuously EXPLORING" to BUILD glasses/canes. No product name, no price, no ship date, no demo unit. Compare: the same article lists their rehab exoskeleton as deployed in hospitals since Jan 2025, so they clearly distinguish shipped vs. research — blind tools are firmly in the research bucket.

CORRECTION 2 (form_factor / category mismatch): The "40g ¥4,299 glasses shipping" cited as evidence is a TRANSLATION + productivity AR-display glasses for SIGHTED users (122-language real-time translation, lip-motion noise reduction, "GlassClaw" cloud AI agent, on-lens subtitle display). It is NOT a blind-assistance device and has NO obstacle-avoidance/navigation/mobility features. Conflating it with the blind product inflates the threat. Also note: this glasses actually launched at MWC 2026 (Mar 2026) with presale 2026-06-15 — newer than the source's framing implied.

CORRECTION 3 (offline): Original said offline "partial." For the blind tools this is unverified-and-unlikely: the perception is built on the cloud-based 讯飞星火 (Spark) LLM doing "复杂场景图像信息提取" (complex-scene image extraction) and the shipping glasses run a cloud "GlassClaw" agent. iFlytek's whole stack is cloud-LLM-first. Setting offline = "no" pending evidence; their architecture is the opposite of our offline wedge.

CORRECTION 4 (overlap): Lowered medium → LOW. Their shipping product targets sighted translation users (different buyer, different job). Their blind product is vaporware-grade R&D and cloud-LLM-based — opposite of our offline + free-base + mobility-safety-grade wedge. Real present-day overlap is low.

CONFIRMED (not refuted): The entity, the CDPF partnership, and the subsidiary are REAL. iFlytek + China Disabled Persons' Federation signed a cooperation agreement May 2024 to build a "通用人工智能助残联合实验室" (General-AI Disability-Assistance Joint Lab); subsidiary 安徽讯飞爱博智能科技 (iFlytek Aibo Intelligent) exists and is registered for the 2025 China Int'l Welfare Expo (Sept 2025). The 2025 AI-accessibility contest and guide-dog-robot R&D references check out. So NOT a hallucination — this is a real, state-backed program, just earlier-stage on blind glasses than the source implied.

why:
Confirmed real, shipping, and well-funded via primary sources. Be My Eyes is a free phone app (volunteer video help + GPT-4-class "Be My AI" image description), now live hands-free on Ray-Ban Meta / Meta AI Glasses across 13+ countries. Both modes (volunteer video, cloud GPT description) require internet — it is NOT offline-capable, confirming the metadata's offline:no. DIRECT OVERLAP IS LOW. Their wedge is reading/scene/help (static visual interpretation); ours is real-time mobility/obstacle avoidance (on-device YOLO + ToF, bone-conduction, fully offline). WHY STILL MEDIUM (not low): two strategic pressures, not feature overlap. (1) FLOOR EFFECT — Be My AI being free makes reading/scene-description a commodity; we must never pitch reading as a paid feature, only bundle it as a free bonus. (2) GIANT DISTRIBUTION — the Ray-Ban/Meta embed (plus Meta's own $299 AI glasses launched 2026-06-23, powered by on-device "Muse Spark") puts a free, ever-improving visual-AI assistant on mainstream hardware millions already wear. That normalizes "AI glasses for blind users" and could expand toward navigation over time. The threat is platform/positioning, not head-to-head mobility today — hence medium, with the watch-item being whether Meta's on-device model ever ships real-time obstacle/mobility safety.
corrections: Funding figure sharpened: not a vague "VC-backed" — Be My Eyes raised a $6.1M Series A+ in Jan 2025 led by Enable Ventures, with National Federation of the Blind (NFB) and ECMC Group's Educational Impact Fund participating, plus non-dilutive enterprise working capital. Added 2026 context: Meta launched own-brand AI glasses at $299 on 2026-06-23 (on-device "Muse Spark" model), deepening the giant-distribution dynamic. Everything else in the original entry (free, no subscription, shipping, startup, GPT-powered Be My AI, low product overlap, medium threat) checks out against primary sources.

name: Be My Eyes (Be My AI + volunteer call)
one_liner: Free app pairing GPT-4-powered instant image descriptions with a live video call to one of millions of sighted human volunteers, 24/7.
form_factor: Smartphone app (iOS + Android); also integrated into some smart glasses
price_usd: Free for blind/low-vision users (enterprise side is paid)
target_users: Blind and low-vision; everyday visual tasks, reading, troubleshooting, shopping
key_features: Be My AI gives detailed GPT-4 image descriptions with follow-up chat; one-tap escalation to free live sighted volunteers via video; volunteer network in 150+ countries. Monetizes via enterprise (Be My Eyes for Work, corporate customer-support/volunteering), keeping the consumer side permanently free.
funding: Raised ~$17.4M total; $6.1M Series A+ in Jan 2025 (Enable Ventures, ECMC, NFB, LiteCap, Samaritan). Enterprise revenue model subsidizes free consumer tier.
why:
Real, dominant, and growing. Be My Eyes is the category default for blind/low-vision visual assistance: ~430K blind users, ~6.1M sighted volunteers, 43M+ requests/yr, 150+ countries, ~200 languages. Be My AI (GPT-4 image description + follow-up chat) and one-tap escalation to a free live volunteer are both confirmed and free, permanently — subsidized by enterprise revenue (Be My Eyes for Work; clients incl. Microsoft, Google, Sony, Spotify, Hilton; 2024 revenue +361%). Funded ~$17.4M total, with a $6.1M Series A+ in Jan 2025 (Enable Ventures lead, ECMC, NFB, LiteCap, Samaritan) at its highest valuation in 10 years. Threat is HIGH on BRAND/DISTRIBUTION/PRICE: they own mindshare and "free forever," which neutralizes our free-basic-tier pitch as a wedge — being free is table stakes, not differentiation, against them. NEW and important: as of 2025 Be My Eyes runs hands-free on Ray-Ban Meta and Oakley Meta smart glasses ("Hey Meta, Be My Eyes"), so they now occupy the smart-glasses form factor too — raising hardware overlap vs. our earlier read. BUT overlap on our actual WEDGE is only MEDIUM, not high: (1) They are 100% online — both Be My AI (cloud GPT-4) and the volunteer call require connectivity; they CANNOT do real-time, safety-critical obstacle avoidance offline. Our offline mobility/avoidance (YOLO+ToF, bone-conduction, sub-second, no network) is a genuinely different capability they do not offer. (2) They are reactive Q&A ("describe this photo"), not continuous low-latency mobility/path guidance — a cloud round-trip is unfit for "step ahead in 1.5m." (3) They ride someone else's hardware (phone, or Meta's glasses) — no depth sensor, no dedicated mobility compute. Net: NOT a competitor on mobility-safety, but a HIGH strategic threat as the incumbent that defines "free AI for the blind," now on glasses, and the obvious first place to bolt on more software features. We must position against them on offline + mobility-safety-grade, not on "free" or "AI describes things."</why_threat_or_not>
<parameter name="corrections">All claims in the input are accurate. Refinements: (1) offline:"no" is correct and fundamental — verified that BOTH Be My AI (cloud GPT-4 on OpenAI servers) and the volunteer video call require an active internet connection; there is no offline mode. This is the key axis where we differ. (2) "also integrated into some smart glasses" should be upgraded from vague to specific and current: as of 2025 Be My Eyes runs natively/hands-free on Ray-Ban Meta and Oakley Meta AI glasses ("Hey Meta, Be My Eyes" for volunteer calls); volunteer-on-glasses was announced at Meta Connect Sept 2024 and expanded in 2025. This materially increases form-factor overlap with us. (3) Funding figures confirmed: ~$17.4M total; $6.1M Series A+ Jan 22 2025, Enable Ventures lead (Gina Kline to board), plus ECMC Educational Impact Fund, NFB, LiteCap, Samaritan; described as highest valuation since founding ~10 yrs ago; non-dilutive working capital from enterprise partners included in the $6.1M. (4) Enterprise model confirmed (Be My Eyes for Work / customer-service + workplace accommodation; 2024 revenue +361%, 43M+ requests/yr). (5) Be My AI launched general availability 2023 (announced Mar 2023 as "Virtual Volunteer," renamed Be My AI, GA rollout from Aug 2023). I'd argue overlap_with_us is more precisely "medium" as scored — high on brand/form-factor/price, low on our mobility wedge — so I kept "medium."</corrections>

name: Aira (Aira Explorer + Access AI)
one_liner: Professional (paid) human visual interpreters on demand, plus a free 'Access AI' image-description feature and free 5-min/48h and venue-sponsored access.
form_factor: Smartphone app (iOS + Android)
price_usd: Paid plans from $26/mo (Silver, 20 min) up to $1,160/mo (Platinum, 880 min); add-on minute packs ~$55/25min. Access AI free for all; non-subscribers get 5 free min / 48h + free at Access Partner venues (Starbucks, Target) + Job Seeker offer (30 free min/24h)
target_users: Blind/low-vision professionals and travelers needing reliable, trained human help for complex tasks
key_features: Trained (not volunteer) agents guide users live through airports, forms, navigation, reading; tiered Silver/Gold/Platinum minute plans; subsidized via 'Aira Offsets' and enterprise/venue sponsorships; Access AI gives free instant AI image descriptions; non-subscribers get 5 free minutes/48h and free access at partner locations.
funding: VC-backed startup; relies on enterprise/venue/government access-partner sponsorships to subsidize per-minute human-agent cost.
why: REAL and actively shipping — Aira Explorer iOS app is v4.13.3 updated June 18 2026, developer "Aira Tech Corp," live on iOS/Android/web. But LOW threat to us and LOW overlap. (1) Different category: Aira's core value is on-demand human visual interpreters (paid per-minute) — a service/labor model, not a hardware/perception product. (2) Cloud/online by design: the entire model is live video streamed to remote agents; it CANNOT work offline. Our wedge is offline, latency-critical mobility safety (obstacle avoidance via on-device YOLO+ToF→bone conduction). A human on a phone call is structurally unfit for real-time "step ahead in 1.5m" warnings. (3) No mobility wedge: Aira does navigation help conversationally (a human describing surroundings), not sub-second collision avoidance. (4) Form factor: smartphone app, no glasses/wearable, no haptic/bone-conduction obstacle layer. (5) Their Access AI overlaps only with our FUTURE optional cloud scene-description tier, not our core. Where they could matter: brand/distribution in the blind community (Access Partner venues, enterprise deals) and as the incumbent "get help with visual tasks" habit. But on our actual moat — offline, free-base, mobility-safety-grade on-device sensing — there is no head-to-head competition. Watch, don't fear.
corrections: 1) PRICE WRONG: claim said "from ~$65/mo for 20 min." Primary source (aira.io/subscriptions) shows the 20-min plan is $26/mo (Silver 1-star), not $65. The ~$65 figure matches only an add-on minute pack (25 min = $55) or old pricing, not the entry subscription. 2) FUNDING STALE/AMBIGUOUS: claim said generic "VC-backed startup." Reality: Aira Tech Corp raised ~$36M (seed + Series B led by JAZZ/Lux/ARCH; total ~$36.1M as of 2020) and was ACQUIRED by Blue Diego Investment Group — it is no longer an independent venture-funded startup. (Note: a $192M figure floating in Tracxn and a $14.5M Series B from AT&T/Intel both belong to "Aira Technologies," an UNRELATED telco-network AI company — do not conflate.) 3) Free tiers in claim are slightly understated but directionally correct (5 free min/48h, Access Partner venues, Access AI free all confirmed; plus Job Seeker 30-min/24h and Intuit small-business offers).

name: Apple Magnifier — Detection Mode (+ VoiceOver/Maps)
one_liner: Free built-in iOS feature using LiDAR + camera + on-device ML for door, people, and furniture detection with image descriptions — but explicitly NOT for navigation.
form_factor: Built into iPhone/iPad (LiDAR models); no extra app or hardware
price_usd: Free (included with iOS on LiDAR-equipped devices)
target_users: Blind and low-vision iPhone users
key_features: On-device Detection Mode finds doors (with attributes, how-to-open, and reads nearby signs/room numbers), detects people with distance, and furniture (occupied chairs/sofas), plus image descriptions — all on-device/offline. VoiceOver and Apple Maps add sound/haptic cues for walking start points. Apple explicitly warns it must NOT be relied on for navigation or in high-risk/emergency situations.
funding: Built and shipped by Apple as a platform accessibility feature; bundled free with the OS — the strongest 'free floor' on iPhone.
why:
Real, shipping, free, no-subscription, fully on-device/offline, and Apple is the strongest possible free floor on the device most blind/low-vision US users already carry. That is the structural reason to take it seriously at medium threat: it anchors willingness-to-pay near zero for the broad describe-my-surroundings category and trains users to expect accessibility for free. But head-to-head overlap with our actual wedge is LOW, by Apple's own design and disclaimer. (1) Apple explicitly tells users NOT to use Detection Mode for navigation or where they could be harmed, so Apple has carved itself OUT of the mobility-safety use case we build for. (2) It is a phone-in-hand point-and-scan interaction, not hands-free continuous wearable sensing while walking; a blind person cannot hold a phone up and sweep it while moving with a cane or dog. (3) It is identification/description (this is a door, a person is 2m away, here is an occupied chair), not continuous real-time obstacle/step/path avoidance with sub-second bone-conduction alerts. (4) Detection Mode is gated to LiDAR Pro/Pro Max models. Our differentiation (hands-free split-form glasses plus bone-conduction, continuous mobility-grade avoidance, freemium with paid cloud) sits in exactly the space Apple disclaims. Where Apple DOES press on us is the future platform layer (OCR, scene description, find-my-things), which are free, so we should never anchor our paid tier on generic scene description. Net: a free floor to design around, not a mobility competitor.</parameter>

<parameter name="corrections">Two framing corrections, both lowering the threat. (1) Detection Mode (doors/people/furniture via LiDAR plus on-device ML) is NOT a navigation/mobility tool. Apple's own warning is explicit and verbatim: Detection Mode should not be relied on in high-risk or emergency situations, in circumstances where you may be harmed or injured, or for navigation. That is the opposite of our mobility wedge. (2) The input entry tags overlap=medium and threat=high, which overstates it. Detection Mode is hardware-gated to iPhone Pro/Pro Max (12 Pro and later) and iPad Pro (2020+) because non-Pro iPhones lack LiDAR; the broad free Magnifier plus image-description features run on all iOS 26 devices, but those are description/reading, not obstacle avoidance. Genuine product overlap with our mobility-safety wedge is LOW, not medium, so threat revised to medium. The feature facts themselves (free, no subscription, on-device/offline, shipping in iOS 26, LiDAR-based, sound/speech/haptic feedback, the navigation disclaimer) are all accurate; the only material inaccuracy was the source's own overlap/threat self-rating.</parameter>

name: Google Maps — Detailed Voice Guidance + Live View
one_liner: Free walking navigation with screen-free detailed voice guidance (on/off-route, turns, busy-intersection warnings) and AR Live View, built with a blind engineer.
form_factor: Smartphone app (iOS + Android)
target_users: All walkers; explicitly co-designed for vision-impaired pedestrians
key_features: Detailed Voice Guidance announces street names, that you're on-route, distance to next turn, heading direction, heads-up for large intersections, and off-route alerts. Live View uses AR + camera to help orient and locate POIs. Outdoor street-level wayfinding only — no obstacle/step detection.
funding: Google platform product; free, no monetization on the accessibility features.
why: Real and shipping, but adjacent-not-overlapping. It solves OUTDOOR STREET-LEVEL WAYFINDING (where to turn), not our wedge — real-time OBSTACLE/STEP AVOIDANCE on the ground in front of you. Detailed Voice Guidance (launched 2019-10-10, World Sight Day, co-designed with Wakana Sugiyama, a legally blind Google Tokyo analyst — confirmed via Google's own blog) announces on-route status, distance-to-turn, heading, busy-intersection cautions, and off-route reroutes. Live View adds AR camera orientation and is actively expanding in 2026 to 1,000+ indoor venues (airports/stations/malls in Berlin, London, Paris, Singapore, Taipei, etc.). Neither does obstacle, step, curb, or low-hanging-hazard detection — explicitly confirmed absent in Google's primary source. Three structural reasons it is not a direct threat: (1) NOT OFFLINE — Google's own help docs state plainly 'When you're offline, transit, bicycling, or walking directions are unavailable'; only DRIVING works offline. Both these accessibility features need connectivity, so they fail exactly where a blind user's safety can't fail. Our offline-first, safety-grade mobility is the differentiator. (2) NO HARDWARE — it's a phone app; user must hold/aim a phone, no hands-free bone-conduction, no ToF depth, no glasses form factor. (3) Different job — it's the macro 'navigate to the cafe' layer; we are the micro 'don't trip on the step 1.5m ahead' layer. They are complementary, not substitutes — a user could run Google Maps for routing AND our glasses for obstacle safety. Threat is medium-low not zero only because Google is a free giant with the brand, distribution, and accessibility credibility to extend downward into hazard-awareness if it ever ships depth-sensing AR glasses (watch Android XR). For now: not a competitor to our wedge.
corrections: Source card claimed offline='partial'. CORRECTED to 'no': Google's official help documentation (support.google.com/maps/answer/6291838) states explicitly that walking, transit, and bicycling directions are ALL unavailable offline — only driving navigation works offline. Therefore both Detailed Voice Guidance (a walking feature) and Live View (camera/AR, needs connectivity) are online-only. The 'partial offline' on the card likely conflated Google Maps' general offline-map download capability (driving-only) with these specific pedestrian/AR accessibility features, which do not function offline at all. Everything else on the card checks out: free, no subscription, shipping, giant-backed (Google platform product, accessibility features not monetized), co-designed with a blind engineer, outdoor wayfinding only, no obstacle/step detection. verified set to 'partly' solely because of the offline error; all other claims confirmed against primary sources.
