name: EyeGuide Vision
one_liner: Free LiDAR/AR navigation app (Nigeria startup) giving voice + escalating-vibration obstacle/collision warnings, human-presence detection, all on-device.
form_factor: Smartphone app (iOS LiDAR; Android via ARCore/sensor fusion)
region: Nigeria/Africa origin; on global app stores
target_users: Blind/low-vision pedestrians in low-resource markets
key_features: Scans surroundings and gives voice prompts ('turn left', 'collision detected') plus vibration that intensifies as obstacles near; reports distance in meters; detects human presence; adds Smart Reader OCR, currency ID, color ID. Processes everything locally (offline, private). The closest philosophical twin to us — offline + free + obstacle/collision + emerging-market focus — but phone-in-hand, no dedicated wearable, very early/solo-founder.
funding: Early-stage solo-founder startup (Charles Ayere, Lagos); no disclosed funding; free.
why:
真实存在且在活跃迭代——不是幻觉。App Store 列表(id6752293641)显示卖家 Charles Ayere、价格 Free、无订阅,iOS v2.0.4 于 2026-06-22 更新;Android 版已上线(用 ARCore/sensor fusion 替代 LiDAR)。多个独立来源(TechCabal 两篇、Techparley、AppleVis)交叉印证。理念上是离我们最近的"双胞胎":离线 + 免费无订阅 + 避障/碰撞警告(语音 + 逐级增强震动)+ 新兴市场视障人群 + 创始人"应像水一样人人可得"的免费哲学。

为什么是 medium 而非 high(三大降级因素):
1) 形态根本不同——它是 phone-in-hand 手机 App,用户得手持手机扫描;我们是免提的穿戴式分体眼镜(~30g 镜框 + 颈挂盒),mobility-safety 场景下"手里拿着手机走路"对盲人是劣势,这是我们的核心差异化。
2) 离线并非全离线——2026-01 TechCabal 实测记者确认:避障/OCR/货币/颜色本地运行,但 Scene Description(场景描述)"需要联网才能最佳工作"。所以它的"fully offline"被夸大,标 offline=partial;我们主张的"基础完整离线"仍是真差异点。
3) 极早期、单人创始人(Lagos 的 Charles Ayere),无披露融资,免费无营收。资源弱、护城河浅。

需要盯防的趋势(为何不降到 low):创始人正在原型化"通过蓝牙连接 EyeGuide 的智能眼镜",用更便宜的超声波传感器(非 LiDAR)面向 Android 用户——这会直接侵入我们的穿戴硬件赛道,且他选了和我们一样的"硬件只覆盖成本、不逐利"定价路线。若他从 App 走到眼镜,重叠度会从 medium 升向 high。实测也暴露其能力上限(过马路判断失败、无法测障碍物深宽、识别精度受光照影响),说明 mobility-safety-grade 仍有空间——这正是我们 wedge 的机会。
corrections:
原 dossier 的修正:
1) offline:原标 "yes(fully offline)" → 实际为 partial。2026-01 TechCabal 记者实测确认 Scene Description 功能需要联网;只有避障/OCR/货币/颜色离线。"everything processed locally/fully offline" 被夸大。
2) form_factor:原标 "Smartphone app, no dedicated wearable" → 需更新。创始人已在原型化通过蓝牙连接的超声波智能眼镜(面向 Android),硬件方向已启动,虽未发布。
3) status 细化:不仅 iOS shipping——Android 版已于 ~2026 初上线(ARCore/sensor fusion,非 LiDAR)。当前 iOS 版本 v2.0.4,2026-06-22 更新,在活跃迭代(最近加了多语言语音 + 本地化距离单位)。
4) overlap_with_us:原标 "high" → 下调为 medium。形态差异(手持手机 vs 免提穿戴眼镜)+ 非全离线,使真实重叠低于"high"。威胁主要来自其未来眼镜计划与免费哲学,而非当前产品形态。
5) price/subscription/存在性:原信息准确,已确认——免费、无订阅、真实存在(非幻觉/refuted)。

name: BeSpecular
one_liner: Free store-and-forward remote-human helper: send a photo + voice question, get a description back from volunteers (asynchronous, not live).
form_factor: Smartphone app
target_users: Blind/low-vision for non-urgent description tasks
key_features: User sends a photo plus a recorded voice message to a volunteer community and gets a spoken/text description back. Asynchronous (not real-time video like Be My Eyes), so not suited to mobility/safety. Listed in 2025-2026 roundups but momentum and current activity are uncertain.
funding: Small startup; activity/maintenance status uncertain — flagged unknown.

name: Ray-Ban Meta + Be My Eyes (Meta)
one_liner: The giant in the room: $300-class mainstream AI glasses with Meta AI + hands-free Be My Eyes integration, marketed directly at blind/low-vision — does NOT do real-time obstacle/mobility but normalizes camera-glasses and owns distribution.
form_factor: Mainstream Ray-Ban/Oakley glasses, camera + open-ear audio (also Display variant)
price_usd: Meta Glasses from $299 (new entry tier, announced Jun 23 2026); Ray-Ban Meta ~$299-379; Meta Ray-Ban Display ~$799
region: USA (Meta); global, Be My Eyes in 13+ countries
target_users: Mainstream consumers; accessibility as a secondary marketed use
key_features: Meta AI scene Q&A, hands-free 'Call a Volunteer' on Be My Eyes (7.7M volunteers), March 2026 expansion to call trusted contacts/brand service teams. Cloud AI, no dedicated obstacle/path detection. Massive retail distribution + brand normalize face-cameras.
why: REAL and shipping at scale — Meta (public mega-cap) with the Be My Eyes accessibility layer is the giant in the room. Threat is high but INDIRECT/strategic, not feature-overlap: it normalizes camera-on-face glasses for blind/low-vision, owns retail distribution + brand, and sets the $300 price anchor consumers expect. The submitted overlap rating of 'medium' is too generous — I downgrade to LOW on the product axis. Verified from primary sources that Meta AI scene description is CLOUD-ONLY (glasses send a photo to Meta's cloud; "you can't use any AI features locally on the glasses," internet required), and there is ZERO real-time obstacle/path/step/curb/mobility detection in any Meta or Be My Eyes material — their value is human volunteers ("Call a Volunteer") + cloud scene Q&A. That is precisely the gap Second Eye occupies: offline, mobility-safety-grade avoidance that works with no connectivity. The danger is not that Meta does what we do — it doesn't — but that (a) "good enough" cloud describe-my-surroundings at $299 with massive distribution caps willingness-to-pay and mindshare, and (b) Meta could add on-device depth/mobility later given hardware reach. Our defensible wedge: offline by design (safety when there's no signal), free unsubscribed mobility base, dedicated ToF+YOLO obstacle pipeline, split-architecture lightweight frame. Compete on safety-grade offline mobility, not on scene description.
corrections: Submitted card is accurate; two refinements. (1) overlap_with_us: 'medium' -> LOW. No product/feature overlap — Meta/Be My Eyes does cloud scene Q&A + human-volunteer video calls, NOT real-time obstacle/mobility detection, which is our entire wedge. (2) Volunteer count: the cited "7.7M volunteers" does NOT appear in the primary March 2026 release; Be My Eyes' own announcement says "nearly one million users worldwide." 7.7M is the commonly-cited historical global volunteer pool — plausible but unconfirmed in the linked sources; treat as soft. (3) Pricing now lower than card states: Meta launched new "Meta Glasses" from $299 on Jun 23 2026 (~$80 below prior Gen 2 entry), sharpening the price anchor. (4) March 2026 expansion confirmed: announced Mar 11 2026 at CSUN — hands-free calls to trusted contacts AND brand service teams (Tesco, Zain, Sony, Clearblue, Amtrak, Hilton); Be My Eyes now on Ray-Ban Meta + Oakley Meta in ~21 markets.

name: .lumen Glasses (dotlumen)
one_liner: Guide-dog-replacement headset (6 cameras, 2 IR lasers, 3 IMUs, forehead haptics) — the most-capable mobility device, but ~€9,999 (~15-20x our price), ~1kg, still pre-commercial/reservations only as of CES 2026.
form_factor: Bulky head-worn headset (~1 kg), forehead haptics + beamformed audio
price_usd: €9,999 (~$11,800), reservations/pre-order only — ships to Romania first (3-6 mo lead time), wider EU early 2026
region: Romania; pre-order US/India pending FDA + CDSCO
funding: Heavily EU-funded: EIC Accelerator ~€9.7M + €5M round (2024-25) + EIT Urban Mobility; CES 2026 Innovation Award (Accessibility)
target_users: Blind users seeking guide-dog-level autonomous navigation
key_features: 6 cameras + 2 IR laser projectors (works after dark) + 3 IMUs + GPS, detects overhead/ground/sunken obstacles, stairs, doors, bus stops, puddles, snow; physically nudges the head via haptics. 400+ testers across 40+ countries. Romania sales early 2026, US/India ~Q3 2026 pending medical certification. Stated mission: blind users pay little/nothing.
why:
REAL and well-funded — not a hallucination. Confirmed across primary sources (dotlumen.com, New Atlas, EIC) — Romanian startup, 400+ testers in 40+ countries, ~1,500 pre-orders, CE certified, demoing at CES 2026, Romania launch early 2026.

KEY OVERLAP (why it matters to us): Same core wedge — MOBILITY / real-time obstacle avoidance for the blind — and, importantly, the SAME two pillars we treat as our differentiators are ALSO true of .lumen: (1) FULLY OFFLINE / 100% on-device processing, no cloud, no internet, no pre-mapped environments (explicitly their design principle); (2) NO subscription, one-time purchase. So "offline + no-subscription" is NOT unique to us against this competitor. It detects stairs, overhangs, sunken obstacles, crosswalks, traffic — the exact mobility-safety category we claim.

WHY ONLY MEDIUM THREAT (the gaps that protect us):
- PRICE: €9,999 / ~$11,800 vs our sub-$700 target — ~17x. Different market entirely (guide-dog replacement for a wealthy/insurance-funded few vs. mass-market affordable aid). Their stated mission is "blind users pay little/nothing," but that depends on reimbursement/subsidy that doesn't exist yet at scale.
- FORM FACTOR: ~1 kg bulky forehead headset, ~2 hr battery, forehead-haptic guidance — opposite of our ~30g lightweight glasses + neck-box split. We win on wearability/all-day use.
- MATURITY: Still pre-commercial (reservations only, ships Romania first); not actually shipping to customers as of CES 2026.
- They are heavier-capability (6 cameras + 2 IR lasers work after dark, head-nudging haptics) — a more advanced device, but a far more expensive, heavier, narrower product.

NET: A credible, better-funded incumbent in the same category that validates the mobility wedge AND the offline+no-sub model — so we cannot lean on "offline + free base" as our sole moat vs them. Our defensible angles are PRICE (~17x cheaper), FORM FACTOR (30g glasses vs 1kg helmet), freemium cloud-enhanced platform path, and bone-conduction vs forehead haptics. Different price tier keeps it medium, not high.</why_threat_or_not>
<parameter name="corrections">Original claim had offline="unknown" and subscription="unknown". CORRECTED: offline=YES (explicitly 100% on-device, no cloud/internet/pre-mapped maps — confirmed by techeblog + offline-architecture writeups citing dotlumen), and subscription=NO (one-time purchase, no subscription mentioned anywhere). Weight ~1kg, ~2hr battery confirmed. Funding confirmed: €5M round (2024, led by Catalyst Romania / EIC Fund / SeedBlink crowdfunding) + €9.3M EIC Accelerator + an €11M grant for autonomous delivery robots (PAD AI) — original's "~€9.7M EIC + €5M round" is materially correct. One caveat: the 'CES 2026 Innovation Award (Accessibility)' is plausible (CES.tech listing + a confirmed Red Dot Design Award exist) but one source covering the CES 2026 demo did NOT mention an award — treat the award as likely-but-not-load-bearing, not fully nailed. Status correctly = pre-launch (reservations/pre-order only, Romania-first early 2026). Region/price all confirmed.</corrections>
<parameter name="threat_level">medium

name: Ara (Strap Technologies)
one_liner: Chest-worn haptic obstacle detector (head/chest/foot levels via 10 actuators) claiming up to 80% fewer hits — direct mobility-job overlap but $2,750, chest form factor, early-stage.
form_factor: Chest-worn device + strap, 6+4 haptic actuators
price_usd: $2,750 (early-bird codes ~-$500; sold in limited batches of 250 with $500–$600 down payment; financing via Affirm/Afterpay ~$58/mo x36)
target_users: Blind/low-vision needing hands-free obstacle warning
key_features: Sensors warn of obstacles at head/chest/foot levels, translated to vibration patterns (not raw data). Claims up to 80% hit reduction. 30-day money-back guarantee. No reading/scene/AI-assistant layer marketed — pure mobility.
why: MEDIUM threat. Real, validated mobility-job overlap: hands-free real-time obstacle warning via haptics, fully offline, no subscription — the same core promise as our mobility wedge, and they're already in market with real testers. That makes them the closest "pure mobility" comp we've found. BUT they are beatable on the exact axes we chose: (a) Price — $2,750 vs our sub-$700 target is a ~4x gap; we win on accessibility for a low-income disability market. (b) Form factor — chest-worn strap device vs our lightweight glasses (~30g) + neck box; glasses are less stigmatizing and free hands/torso. (c) Sensing — they use ultrasonic/LiDAR only; our camera + on-device YOLO unlocks the platform roadmap (OCR, scene description, traffic-light/object ID, find-my-thing, future cloud agent) that they explicitly do NOT market. (d) Capital — ~$1.3M seed and batch-of-250 supply suggests they can't out-build or out-distribute a funded competitor. Net: validates the problem and the haptic-mobility approach (de-risks us), but does NOT own our differentiation. Watch them as the category benchmark; they are not a moat-holder. Caveat: their "offline + no-subscription" overlaps ours, so our messaging must lead with price + glasses form + the platform/vision roadmap, NOT with "offline/no-subscription" alone.
corrections:
REAL company — not a hallucination. But several record fields were wrong or unverified:
1) offline: input "unknown" → confirmed YES. Ara is standalone and "does not require an app or connection to function" (multiple sources). They are offline too — this is NOT a differentiator for us.
2) subscription: input "unknown" → confirmed NO. One-time purchase; the "$58/mo" is Affirm/Afterpay financing, not a subscription. Our "no-subscription" wedge does not differentiate vs Ara (only price/form-factor does).
3) funding: input "VC-backed, Crunchbase-listed" overstates it. Crunchbase/Tracxn show only ~$1.3M seed (Apr 2021, led by Platform Capital), no later rounds found; also International Accelerator portfolio. Tiny, capital-constrained — not a well-funded threat.
4) status: input "shipping" → more precisely batch/pre-order. Site shows "Sold Out," sells in limited 250-unit batches with down payments; not freely shipping off the shelf.
5) sensors: ultrasonic + LiDAR + AI haptic translation (NOT a camera/on-device-YOLO vision stack like ours; no scene/OCR/AI-assistant layer — pure mobility).
6) region: HQ Austin TX (founded 2018) + Guadalajara, Mexico dev office — not "Mexico/US" as a market.
7) recency: strongest signals are 2024 (Nov 2024 batch, Cannes 2024 campaign). No clear 2025–2026 shipment/news/funding found — site live but momentum data is ~18mo stale.
8) claims (80% fewer mid/upper-body hits, injury 36%→6%, +11% distance, +19% speed, ~250 testers / ~17k hours) are self-reported, not independently verified.

name: Shanghai multimodal visual-assistance system (Nature Machine Intelligence)
one_liner: Academic prototype (4 Shanghai universities, Nature MI 2025) that is the closest combination to us: camera between the eyebrows + bone-conduction obstacle cues + wrist artificial-skin haptics + smart insoles — research-only, not a product, but proves and publishes our exact approach.
form_factor: Head-mounted camera (between eyebrows) + bone-conduction + wrist e-skin + smart insoles
funding: Academic; published Nature Machine Intelligence, Apr 2025
target_users: Research subjects; blind/partially-sighted navigation
key_features: AI route-planning from a head-cam detects overhead/ground-level/sunken obstacles and slopes via global-threshold + ground-interval method; bone-conduction directional audio + stretchable wrist e-skin vibration for lateral avoidance; smart insoles. Tested with humanoid robots + blind participants; navigation speed reached cane-comparable and pathfinding more efficient than cane after training. No commercial product, but a teardown/borrow source and a signal that big Chinese hardware could productize this combo cheaply.
why: Real and confirmed against the primary source: "Human-centred design and fabrication of a wearable multimodal visual assistance system," Nature Machine Intelligence Vol 7 pp 627-638, published 14 Apr 2025, corresponding author Leilei Gu (Shanghai Jiao Tong Univ). It is the closest published approach to us on APPROACH — RGB-D camera on a 3D-printed glasses frame + on-device YOLOv8 + bone-conduction directional audio + stretchable e-skin haptics + triboelectric smart insoles — and it independently validates our exact mobility/offline thesis. CRITICAL CONFIRMATION: it runs fully on-device, "all computations performed locally without the need for a remote server... functional without cloud dependency" (Physics World) — the input field had offline as "unknown"; corrected to YES. This strengthens our market premise (a top-tier journal proves offline mobility-safety works) but it is NOT a competitive threat to ship against: it is an academic prototype with NO product, NO price, NO company, NO announced commercialization path, NO subscription. There is nothing for a blind user to buy. Hence threat_level downgraded from the input's "medium" to LOW. The residual risk is indirect: it is a published, FigShare/GitHub-open teardown/borrow source and a signal that a Chinese hardware giant could productize this combo cheaply — that is a 12-24 month watch-item, not a head-to-head competitor today. Overlap stays HIGH (same wedge, same sensing stack, same offline stance), which is exactly why it is more useful to us as prior-art validation than as a competitor.
corrections: 1) Institutions: input says "4 Shanghai universities." Actually Shanghai Jiao Tong Univ + Fudan + East China Normal (Shanghai) + Shanghai AI Lab, PLUS Hong Kong University of Science & Technology (HONG KONG, not Shanghai). Not all Shanghai; HKUST is a key collaborator. (Physics World's "four Shanghai universities" phrasing is imprecise; Xinhua + Nature confirm HKUST.) 2) offline: input "unknown" -> corrected to YES, explicitly on-device/no remote server (Physics World), uses YOLOv8. 3) Components nuance: e-skin haptics are on finger (ToF sensor) + wrist actuator (18ms response), not "wrist only"; smart insoles are a VR-based TRAINING component, not a deployed navigation sensor. Camera is RGB-D on a 3D-printed glasses frame; "between the eyebrows" is approximately right but not explicitly stated as such in primary sources. 4) status: "pre-launch" overstates intent — it is an academic prototype with no announced product/commercialization; treat as research, not a product in the pipeline. 5) Exact title: "Human-centred design and fabrication of a wearable multimodal visual assistance system"; lead/corresponding author Leilei Gu (not "Jian Tang" as PI — Jian Tang and Yi Zhu are co-first authors, Gu is corresponding).

name: Envision Glasses (original, Google Glass-based)
one_liner: $2,499 reading/scene glasses built on Google's now-EOL'd Glass Enterprise Edition 2 — orphaned-hardware cautionary tale (don't build on a platform a giant can kill); company now pivoting to the cheaper Ally Solos.
form_factor: Google Glass EE2 base (discontinued by Google), camera + bone-conduction/speaker
funding: VC-backed; also ships well-regarded free Envision phone app
target_users: Blind/low-vision; reading, scene, faces, calls
key_features: Instant/Scan/Batch text reading, scene/face/cash/color, Call-a-Companion/Aira. Hardware platform (Glass EE2) discontinued by Google Mar 2023, support ended Sep 2023 — entire BOM orphaned by supplier. Ally AI added 2024-25. Now superseded by their own $399 Ally Solos.

name: NuEyes (Pro4 LV / e2+)
one_liner: Another low-vision magnification + OCR headset (12x zoom, voice control) for residual-sight users — magnifier category like eSight/IrisVision, not blind-mobility; low overlap.
form_factor: Smart-glasses/headset magnifier (ODG-based heritage)
target_users: Low-vision (residual sight) + deaf (captions) — NOT the blind
key_features: Up to 12x variable magnification, contrast/color enhancement, OCR text-to-speech, voice command control. Reading mail, faces, TV. Magnification job, not obstacle/mobility.

name: SolidddVision (Soliddd)
one_liner: CES-2025-debut VR-style smart glasses using a fly's-eye lens array to route images to undamaged retina for macular-degeneration users — a vision-correction/low-vision device, orthogonal to blind mobility; low overlap.
form_factor: VR-style headset, proprietary multi-lens 'fly's eye' array
target_users: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) / low-vision with residual sight
key_features: Lens arrays project multiple images to non-damaged retina areas so the brain reconstructs a full-field image; aimed at true vision correction, not navigation. Not for the totally blind.

name: Seeing AI (Microsoft) / Google Lookout
one_liner: Free first-party phone apps (Microsoft, Google) for reading/scene/products/faces — own the information-access job at $0; phone-in-hand, not real-time walking. The free baseline that killed paid reading hardware.
form_factor: Phone app (Seeing AI iOS+Android; Lookout Android)
target_users: Blind/low-vision; reading, scene, currency, products, faces
key_features: Free OCR, scene/product/face/currency description, channel-based UI. Phone-in-hand — cannot do hands-free real-time obstacle detection while caning. Free baseline that commoditized reading/scene; harvest as proof reading is a free bonus, not a wedge.

name: OKO (AYES)
one_liner: Free phone app that reads pedestrian traffic-light signals via the camera (Apple Design Award 2024) — a single, specific mobility-adjacent job done free on the phone; a feature we should plan to absorb (traffic-light recognition is on our roadmap), not a hardware rival.
form_factor: Phone app (point camera at crossing)
funding: Backed; 2024 Apple Design Award (Inclusivity) + App Store Award
target_users: Blind/low-vision pedestrians at signalized crossings
key_features: Camera-based pedestrian-signal (walk/don't-walk) recognition with audio + haptic + visual cues; also maps. Free. Phone-in-hand at the crossing. Directly maps to a feature on our software roadmap (traffic-light recognition) — borrow the use case, beat it with hands-free.

name: Horus / Eyra
one_liner: Dead under-capitalized predecessor that is ALMOST our exact architecture a decade early: stereo cameras + on-device NVIDIA Tegra GPU + bone-conduction, ~$2,000 — raised only ~$900K, never industrialized, vanished by ~2019. Lesson: buzz != fundable demand; cheap BOM or big raise required.
form_factor: Head-band with 2 stereo cameras + bone-conduction, tethered to pocket compute unit
target_users: Blind/low-vision; obstacle, faces, objects, read-aloud, scene
key_features: Stereo obstacle/nav + face/object recognition + read-aloud on on-device Tegra K1 GPU, bone-conduction output — structurally our concept (stereo + on-device + bone-conduction). 'Early access' Oct 2016 never scaled; under-capitalized for a custom dual-camera + GPU + tethered wearable at ~$2k into a small market. (Note: 'pivoted to Eyeware' is a verified mix-up; treat as simply dead.)

name: Microsoft Soundscape
one_liner: Dead despite being free and loved: Microsoft Research's 3D-audio ambient-awareness nav app, discontinued 2023 when Microsoft killed its Azure backend (bricking even offline-feeling use). Lesson: a benefactor's side project + cloud-backend you don't own = a kill switch. Our fully-offline design is the antidote.
form_factor: iOS phone app, spatial-audio cues
target_users: Blind/low-vision ambient awareness + wayfinding
key_features: 3D spatial-audio cues tied to POIs/intersections. Pulled 3 Jan 2023; installs stopped working 30 Aug 2023 when the Azure backend shut. Open-sourced; community fork VoiceVista carries it on. Killed not for lack of love but because Big Tech assistive = side project + cloud kill-switch.

name: Google Project Guideline
one_liner: Research-only, then open-sourced (2023): Pixel + chest harness + headphones keeps a blind runner on a painted line via on-device ML. Google did assistive as a research/PR project and walked away. Lesson: don't model Big Tech as a market-maker — harvest their open code instead.
form_factor: Google Pixel phone + chest harness + headphones
region: USA (Google Research + Guiding Eyes for the Blind)
target_users: Blind runners/walkers on a marked line
key_features: On-device ML keeps a blind runner on a painted guideline with audio feedback — no cloud, no pre-map. Open-sourced 21 Nov 2023; never a consumer product. Confirms on-device perception works AND that Google won't ship a competing product. Borrow the code, not the market fear.

name: IBM NavCog
one_liner: Research-grade indoor turn-by-turn nav using Bluetooth beacons + smartphone (Carnegie Mellon / IBM) — accurate, but depends on beacons installed in each venue (infrastructure you don't control). Never a shipping consumer product; lesson mirrors BLAID.
form_factor: Smartphone app + venue-installed BLE beacons
target_users: Blind indoor wayfinding (campuses, airports)
key_features: Turn-by-turn indoor nav + semantic POI cues (doorways, shops) via off-the-shelf BLE beacons + phone localization. NavCog3 'in the wild' deployments. Requires beacon infrastructure per building — same map/infra-dependency trap as BLAID. Research, not a product.

name: BrainPort V100 (Wicab)
one_liner: Effectively gone from the West: a $10,000 tongue electro-tactile display (camera to a 400-electrode 'lollipop' on the tongue) — FDA-cleared 2015 but killed by a brutal months-long learning curve + price. Successor only in China. Lesson: sensory substitution must be learnable in minutes.
form_factor: Camera + tongue 'lollipop' electrode array
target_users: Blind users (adjunct to cane)
key_features: Camera image painted on the tongue via ~400 electrodes; positioned as cane adjunct. Steep learning curve + $10k price = adoption death. 'Vision Pro' successor listed only-in-China; promised US/EU return lapsed ~3.5 yrs. Our bone-conduction 'obstacle ahead' must be learnable in minutes, not months.

name: BuzzClip (iMerciv)
one_liner: Discontinued/pivoted: a ~$249 clip-on ultrasonic obstacle detector whose Toronto team concluded software was the real business and pivoted to a pedestrian-nav app (MapinHood). Same warning as Sunu: detector hardware alone isn't a company.
form_factor: Clip-on ultrasonic detector, haptic feedback
target_users: Blind/low-vision upper-body obstacle detection
key_features: Clip-on ultrasonic upper-body obstacle detection via vibration. iMerciv pivoted to MapinHood (pedestrian nav app); imerciv.com shows maintenance. The hardware team decided the software/nav was the real business.

name: Mainstream consumer AI glasses (INMO GO3, Halliday, Looktech, Loomos, RayNeo)
one_liner: The crowded low-end wave: sub-$200-$300 general-purpose AI glasses (ChatGPT/GPT-4o, displays, capture) increasingly marketing 'helps the blind' — NOT mobility-grade and not blind-built, but they drive face-camera normalization, price-anchoring pressure, and could bolt on accessibility cheaply.
category: Mainstream consumer AI glasses (watch / price pressure)
form_factor: Lightweight everyday AI glasses (some with display), camera + open-ear audio
price_usd: Loomos ~$189-199 (KS) / Looktech ~$209 (KS, $349 MSRP) / Halliday ~$399 (KS, $489 retail) / INMO GO3 $499 (KS, $599 MSRP) — NOT $150-300 as claimed / RayNeo X3 Pro ~$1,099-1,299 (not $1,500)
region: China (INMO, RayNeo, Looktech) + US crowdfunding (Loomos, Halliday)
target_users: Mainstream consumers; some blog-marketed to blind/low-vision
key_features: Cloud LLM Q&A, scene description, capture, some with invisible/AR displays + navigation overlays. General-purpose, cloud-dependent, no real-time obstacle/path detection and not built with/for the blind. Relevant as price-anchor and face-camera normalization, and as a pool that could add accessibility features fast.
why: All five products are REAL and shipping/in-fulfillment (none hallucinated). They are a genuine crowded low-end consumer wave, and the original threat logic holds: relevant as price-anchor and face-camera-normalization pressure, NOT as direct competitors. The "increasingly marketing 'helps the blind'" framing is the weakest, most exaggerated part of the claim: I verified RayNeo's "AI glasses for blind" page is a category-education SEO blog, not a product marketed as a mobility aid — it even redirects blind users to a dedicated nav competitor (.lumen) and concedes hybrid cloud reliance. NONE of these ships real-time obstacle/path detection as a mobility-safety feature; they offer cloud scene-description/translation/capture for general consumers. So the moat (offline + mobility-safety-grade + blind-built + free-no-subscription) is intact. Overlap correctly rated LOW. Threat correctly MEDIUM: the real risk is (a) price anchoring — consumers/distributors comparing our sub-$700 device to $200 GPT-4o glasses, and (b) one of these well-funded players bolting on an accessibility mode cheaply. Note our spec sheet should NOT lean on 'we have a camera on glasses' as differentiation — that's now fully normalized by this wave.
corrections: 1) PRICE: claim says '~$150-300 (Loomos <$200, Halliday, Looktech, INMO GO3)' — INMO GO3 is $499 KS / $599 MSRP (a translation+display device, NOT sub-$300); Halliday is $399 KS / $489 retail (above $300). Only Loomos (~$189-199) and Looktech (~$209) are genuinely in the $150-300 band. RayNeo X3 Pro is ~$1,099-1,299, not ~$1,500. 2) OFFLINE: claim marks the whole cluster 'offline: no' — partly wrong. INMO GO3 has a dedicated NPU with on-device OFFLINE translation for 9 languages (its core feature works offline); the others (Loomos/Looktech/Halliday) are indeed cloud-dependent for AI. So 'offline: partial' is more accurate at the cluster level — but importantly, NONE has offline mobility/obstacle safety, which is what matters for us. 3) INMO GO3 timeline: it launched on Kickstarter April 2026 (not 2025) and is the newest entrant; it's a translation/AR-display product, the least 'helps the blind'-relevant of the five. 4) The 'helps the blind' marketing claim is real only as thin SEO blog content (RayNeo), not as shipped mobility products — do not overstate this as a competitive accessibility push.

name: KRETA (克里特 / Kreta Cloud Tech, 克里特云科)
one_liner: Chinese hardware brand on its 3rd-gen blind-navigation glasses; camera + AI road-condition recognition cued via voice/vibration — our exact mobility wedge from a low-BOM house.
form_factor: Smart glasses (also a V31 cycling-glasses line)
price_usd: unknown (China consumer AI-glasses pricing band ~RMB 398-4999 in market; KRETA-specific not published)
region: China (Dongguan Fenggang — NOTE: prompt said Shenzhen; verified HQ is Dongguan)
target_users: Blind / low-vision; also general consumer AI-glasses buyers (cycling)
funding: unknown; multiple invention patents in blind-nav and vision-to-text
key_features: Evolved from LiDAR to vision-space algorithm; camera identifies objects/distance/speed and converts image-to-sound for self-driving-style obstacle avoidance; road-sign/hazard warning (partners Doubao, iFlytek); voice + vibration interaction. CEO Zhang Jiawei (张家炜).

name: Voxsee (Zhejiang University team / 浙江大学)
one_liner: 2025 China Int'l University Student Innovation Competition GOLD for a neck-worn AI device for the blind delivering voice + vibration cues — same split-body neck-hung form factor and haptic channel as Second Eye V1.
form_factor: Neck-worn (颈挂) device — split-body, voice + vibration
target_users: Blind / visually impaired
funding: unknown (university competition winner; national media attention — People's Daily, Xinhua, China Youth Daily)
key_features: Inclusive-level accessible smart assistive device; conveys info via voice + vibration to 'build a visual world' with sound. Built first-code-to-prototype in a few months. Founders He Yilin (accounting UG) + Chen Yihao (microelectronics UG). Credible academic-to-startup spin-out pipeline; signal that others are converging on our exact architecture.

name: Innovega (eMacula / iOptik)
one_liner: Contact-lens-coupled display eyewear that enhances residual sight for low-vision; ~$25M raised (Tencent led a round); Founder Series presold 100+ pairs at $2,950, commercial delivery targeted early 2027.
category: Low-vision display (novel optics: contact lens + eyewear microdisplay)
form_factor: Spectacle microdisplay + iOptik smart contact lens
target_users: Low-vision (residual sight) — NOT blind
funding: ~$25M total; Tencent led an early $3M round; targeting $10-20M round in 2026; also StartEngine raise
key_features: iOptik nano-optic contact lens lets wearer focus simultaneously on real world + near-eye display; Gen One captures scene via camera and projects enhanced image onto transparent displays. A different optical bet than eSight/OxSight HMDs. Relevant only to the low-vision SKU we are choosing NOT to enter; shows fresh capital in that lane.

name: OxSight (Onyx / Crystal) — now in liquidation
one_liner: Oxford spin-out making OLED low-vision glasses (Onyx/Crystal) for AMD/RP/Stargardt; OXSIGHT LTD entered creditors' voluntary liquidation — a fresh European post-mortem for the 'priced-for-nobody low-vision HMD, no payer' kill pattern.
category: Low-vision display HMD — AND graveyard entry
form_factor: OLED sight-enhancing smart glasses (Onyx = peripheral, Crystal/Prism = central)
price_usd: historically multi-thousand (ex-demo units now on eBay/VisionAid; firm no longer trading)
target_users: Low-vision (AMD, RP, Stargardt) — residual sight
key_features: ML/deep-learning CV platform enhancing remaining central or peripheral vision. NOTE: prompt said '2024+ liquidation' — verified the wind-up resolution / creditors' meeting was actually late SEPT 2023 (The Gazette notice 4442769); company is in liquidation, but date is 2023 not 2024. A European data point alongside OrCam's exit and eSight's distress — reinforces our 'low-vision HMD with no payer dies' rule.

name: IrisVision Live 2.0 (discontinued 2024)
one_liner: Samsung-VR-headset-based low-vision magnifier; the Live 2.0 hardware line was DISCONTINUED and the company pivoted to the tethered-phone Inspire + software — another 'built on someone else's hardware platform' casualty.
form_factor: VR headset with mounted smartphone (Live 2.0); successor Inspire = lightweight headset + tethered phone (172g)
price_usd: discontinued (Live 2.0 no longer sold; warranties honored)
target_users: Low-vision — residual sight
key_features: Phone-on-headset magnifier (50MP camera, octa-core). Live 2.0 hardware discontinued; pivoted to Inspire (tethered phone) + software. Same failure mode as Envision-on-Google-Glass: depending on another firm's hardware platform. Reinforces our 'own your BOM' rule with a 2024 example. NOTE: you already list IrisVision Inspire; this is the discontinuation update on the Live line.

name: Acesight / Acesight VR (Zoomax)
one_liner: Zoomax's wearable electronic low-vision glasses — a lower-priced, higher-volume eSight competitor cross-shopped against eSight/NuEyes/IrisVision; the cheap Chinese HMD most likely to undercut any future low-vision SKU we'd build.
form_factor: Wearable VR-style e-glasses (<1 lb) + handheld controller; Acesight S = lighter smart-glasses variant
price_usd: unknown (positioned as 'most affordable wearable'; $100 non-refundable return fee; price not published in results)
target_users: Low-vision (20/200, tunnel vision, AMD, RP, cataract, glaucoma) — residual sight
key_features: 48MP camera, 65° FOV, 1.1x-15x magnification, 60Hz, 2-3h battery, head strap. Cross-shopped favorably vs eSight on picture quality/ease. The price-floor threat for the low-vision-display category we are deliberately avoiding.

name: Zoomax (parent) low-vision e-glasses
one_liner: Major Hangzhou low-vision magnifier maker (parent of Acesight) pushing hands-free wearable e-glasses — the cost-down Chinese price-floor setter for the low-vision-display market we are not entering.
form_factor: E-glasses + handheld/desktop/portable video magnifiers
target_users: Low-vision — residual sight
key_features: Built-in auto-focus camera magnifies onto screen with color/contrast/brightness modes. Parent brand behind Acesight. Worth tracking as the adjacent-market price floor, not a direct rival.

name: HumanWare StellarTrek (2025 update)
one_liner: Deep-pocketed, dealer-networked blindness-AT incumbent; April-2025 update added a book reader + pedestrian TRAFFIC-LIGHT detection (beta) — directly overlapping two of our roadmap items (traffic-light recognition + OCR reading).
form_factor: Handheld AI GPS/OCR device (successor to Victor Reader Trek)
price_usd: unknown (premium AT pricing; sold via dealer network & Amazon)
target_users: Blind / visually impaired
key_features: AI GPS + OCR; identifies addresses/entrances/landmarks with audio guidance for the last 40 ft; April-2025 added integrated book reader (Bookshare) and beta pedestrian traffic-light detection. The serious distribution-channel competitor for any reading/nav features we ship — they already reach our buyers through dealers.

name: OTON Glass
one_liner: Japanese text-to-speech reading glasses (camera → cloud OCR → audio, with remote-human fallback); validates the Japan market and reading-as-bonus job — and is a cautionary cloud-OCR-dependency case for our OCR module.
form_factor: Glasses with twin cameras + earpiece (Raspberry Pi compute)
target_users: Dyslexia, low-vision, foreign-language readers
key_features: Stare at text + blink to trigger; camera → cloud OCR → audio; unrecognized images routed to a remote human worker. Validates Japan + reading-as-bonus; cautionary case for cloud-OCR dependency on a platform OCR module.

name: Pantograph 'Guide Glass' (ガイドグラス)
one_liner: Japanese wearable remote-guidance system sharing the user's view + audio + location with a remote human (an Aira-style model, localized to Japan) — the regional incumbent in the human-in-the-loop lane to map before any Japan entry.
form_factor: Glasses-type wearable + remote operator console
target_users: Blind / visually impaired
funding: unknown (Pantograph Inc., established Japanese dev house)
key_features: Shares wearer's field-of-view, audio and location with a remote third party who provides hands-free guidance — Japan-localized Aira analog. Partner/competitor to map before any Japan entry.

name: Lighthouse Tech (TAMI)
one_liner: Funded Swiss startup with TAMI smart-eyewear: front-facing radar detects above-waist obstacles and cues via low-latency haptics — an active, funded competitor in our EXACT mobility wedge, in a fashion frame.
form_factor: Smart eyewear frame with front-facing A121 radar + haptic feedback
price_usd: unknown (pre-/early-market; bringing to market post-2024 funding)
target_users: Blind / visually impaired (complements white cane)
funding: Venture Kick (CHF 150K stage-2; part of broader CHF 500K seed); plus a €160K boost (Tech.eu, Oct 2024); Acconeer/USI ties
key_features: Acconeer A121 radar continuously scans for obstacles above the waist; lateralized haptics (vibration side = obstacle position, amplitude = distance) — radar not camera/YOLO. NOTE: you list 'TAMI (Lighthouse Tech)' as a name only; this is the full card. Radar-in-a-fashion-frame is a real technical alternative to our camera+ToF; a head-to-head Swiss mobility rival worth a full card.

name: AT&T CarePlus (assistive smart-glasses program)
one_liner: Carrier-as-distribution-and-financing channel: AT&T CarePlus reimburses up to $6,000/lifetime for assistive smart glasses from Aira/eSight/NuEyes/OrCam — not a product but a channel competitor AND a potential route to our buyers.
form_factor: Reimbursement program (channel, not hardware)
price_usd: up to $6,000 lifetime reimbursement (service + SIM + glasses)
target_users: Low/no-vision AT&T members (physician certification required)
key_features: Reimburses one pair of assistive smart glasses from four named vendors; prior approval + physician cert required; no repair/service-fee coverage. Shows how rivals reach blind/low-vision buyers via a carrier — a channel we could pursue or be locked out of. Our competitive map currently omits the payer/carrier channel entirely.

name: Limitless Pendant (acquired by Meta, Dec 2025)
one_liner: AI memory pendant (ex-Rewind) ACQUIRED BY META on Dec 5 2025 and being sunset — direct evidence for strategy rule #2: the standalone AI-wearable category is being absorbed by Meta/Amazon.
form_factor: Wearable AI pendant (conversation recorder)
price_usd: no longer sold (existing owners moved to free Unlimited plan; 1 yr support)
target_users: Productivity / memory (not blindness-specific)
key_features: Hardware sales ended on acquisition; Rewind Mac app capture disabled Dec 19 2025; tech to be folded into Meta's own AI wearables; service pulled from EU/UK/China/etc. NOTE: you list Limitless — the load-bearing update is the Meta acquisition + sunset. Combined with Bee→Amazon, sharp cautionary data point that a single-function wearable is a feature, not a company.

name: Bee (Pioneer) — acquired by Amazon, Jul 2025
one_liner: $49.99 always-listening AI wristband ACQUIRED BY AMAZON (announced Jul 22 2025), now an Alexa data-gathering prototype — the independent AI-pendant thesis collapsed into Big Tech within a year.
form_factor: Fitbit-like wristband, always-listening + mic
target_users: Productivity (summaries, to-dos, reminders) — not blindness-specific
key_features: Passively listens, turns ambient conversation into reminders/summaries/to-dos; now being evolved inside Amazon as an Alexa companion. NOTE: you list Bee/Amazon — the confirmed Jul-2025 acquisition is the key update. Reinforces 'single-function wearable is a feature, not a company' with a 2025 example fresher than Sunu/Humane.

name: Meta 'Live AI' + Be My Eyes (2026 expansion)
one_liner: Meta moving from one-shot scene description toward CONTINUOUS hands-free assistance (Live AI, no wake word) + Be My Eyes now routing to brand service teams (Sony, Hilton, Amtrak, Tesco) at no cost on $299-$499 glasses — the single most important competitor trajectory to track.
form_factor: Ray-Ban Meta / Oakley Meta glasses + Meta AI + Be My Eyes
price_usd: $299 (Ray-Ban Meta) / $499 (Oakley Vanguard); accessibility features free to glasses owner
target_users: Blind / low-vision (among broad consumer base)
key_features: Live AI = continuous, hands-free, no-wake-word scene understanding (direction of OUR wedge); Be My Eyes expansion (announced CSUN, Mar 11 2026) routes hands-free video to trusted contacts AND brand service teams across Tesco, Zain, Sony, Clearblue, Amtrak, Hilton. Cloud-dependent, so still can't do real-time offline mobility — but advancing toward continuous assistance faster than our snapshot implied. The trajectory to watch most closely.

name: Solos (AirGo V2 / Ally Solos)
one_liner: Independent affordable AI-glasses maker (AirGo line, swappable frames, open AirGo SDK) at $249-$299 positioning as an open platform — the '$300 general AI-glasses entrant that adds an obstacle mode' our product-direction doc names as Path-A's key threat.
category: Consumer AI glasses w/ accessibility (edge platform)
form_factor: Modular smart glasses (swappable frames), camera + open-ear audio
target_users: General consumer + accessibility (blind/low-vision via Envision Ally + SDK partners)
key_features: AirGo V2 (CES 2026, $299): SolosChat 3.0 + expanded SDK, multimodal AI routing to ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini/DeepSeek, Full-HD streaming; glasses send audio/images to phone for processing. SDK partners: Envision (Ally), Deutsche Telekom, HK PolyU. NOTE: you list 'Ally Solos' as an Envision collab — Solos itself deserves its own card as the affordable open AI-glasses platform threat. Cloud/phone-dependent, so no offline mobility today.

name: Mentra (MentraOS / AugmentOS)
one_liner: Open-source OS + app store for smart glasses ('the Android for smart glasses', $8M raised, YC/MIT-backed) that lets anyone bolt a free 'obstacle/describe' app onto commodity glasses — the platform route that could commoditize our software layer.
form_factor: Open-source OS + SDK + app store (runs on multiple manufacturers' glasses)
target_users: Developers, manufacturers, end users (incl. accessibility apps)
funding: $8M raised; YC company; MIT Media Lab tie; CEO Cayden Pierce
key_features: MentraOS 2.0 = open ecosystem + SDK; apps run simultaneously with real-time sensor/context; already powers live captions, translation, AI assistant, camera. 'One app runs on supported glasses from multiple makers.' The SDK/glasses-OS route that could let a free obstacle/describe app run on cheap glasses — commoditizing our software moat. None of our platform entries covered an open glasses-OS competitor.

name: BrainPort (Wicab) Vision Pro
one_liner: Tongue-electrode sensory-substitution device (V100 successor); the un-mined nuance is the West/China availability split — sharpens our learnability/price kill-pattern, but the 'China-only' claim is UNVERIFIED.
form_factor: Glasses-mounted camera + tongue electrode array (electrotactile)
region: China (claimed) / US (Wicab is US-based; CE 2013, FDA 2015)
target_users: Blind (incl. trauma-blinded)
key_features: Camera → electrotactile 'image on the tongue'; long learning curve, five-figure price. NOTE: COULD NOT VERIFY the prompt's specific claim that the Vision Pro successor is now sold ONLY in China while US/EU lapsed — Wicab's US site still lists Vision Pro and a US/Lighthouse Guild study is recent; treat 'China-only' as unconfirmed. You already have BrainPort V100 in the graveyard; the broader lesson (where five-figure sensory-substitution survives) holds regardless.

name: Academic offline edge-vision blind-nav stacks (YOLO + depth, Snapdragon/Hailo)
one_liner: A dense 2024-26 research literature reproduces our EXACT recipe — on-device YOLO + depth → audio/haptic, fully offline, real-time — proving the moat isn't the detector but distribution/trust/data.
form_factor: Research prototypes (edge boards: Snapdragon, Hailo-8L + Raspberry Pi, Jetson)
target_users: Blind / visually impaired (research subjects)
key_features: Multiple 2024-26 papers (YOLO-OD/MDPI Sensors, YOLOv8 + stereo + DeepSORT, YOLO-Extreme for fog, 7-model YOLO outdoor benchmarks) report 90%+ accuracy real-time obstacle detection for the blind. NOTE: the specific 'sub-320ms on Snapdragon, 100% collision-avoidance field trial' figure was not pinned to one paper in search, but the category is unambiguously real and reproducible. Commoditization-of-the-core risk: our defensibility must come from platform + community + data, not the CV pipeline.

name: biped 'Smart Glasses' (biped.ai)
one_liner: Our nearest funded mobility competitor (Honda-AI self-driving stack on a shoulder vest) is publicly broadening to 'best mobility devices' positioning — watch for form-factor convergence toward glasses, which would erode our differentiation.
form_factor: Shoulder-worn vest TODAY (3x RealSense D430 depth cams + compute + battery); glasses form factor = NOT confirmed as a shipping product
target_users: Blind / visually impaired
funding: VC-backed; Honda Research Institute co-developed AI
key_features: NOA fuses turn-by-turn GPS + visual data; bone-conduction alerts; positioned as 'most comprehensive 2025 mobility solution.' NOTE: you already list biped NOA. I could NOT verify a shipping/announced biped glasses-form-factor product — current public material still centers the shoulder vest and broad 'best mobility devices' content marketing. Flag as a convergence to monitor, not a confirmed glasses launch. If verified, it would erode our form-factor edge.

name: Oakley Meta Vanguard (+ rumored 'Celeste')
one_liner: Meta's expanding glasses line — Oakley Meta Vanguard sport model shipping at $499 with up to 9h battery + always-on Meta AI; each cheaper/longer-battery generation narrows the gap to a usable always-on assistant for the blind.
form_factor: Sport AI sunglasses (Oakley Meta Vanguard); 12MP / 122° FOV / 3K video
target_users: Athletes/consumers; accessibility via Meta AI + Be My Eyes
key_features: Vanguard launched Oct 21 2025, $499, up to 9h battery (+36h case), Garmin fitness integration, 'Hey Meta'/tap activation. NOTE: 'Celeste' (rumored accessibility-leaning next Ray-Ban Meta with longer battery / always-on AI) did NOT surface in search — treat as an unconfirmed codename/rumor; Vanguard is the verified shipping data point. Tracking Meta's cadence matters: each generation closes the gap to an always-on blind assistant at $299-$499.
